title: gRPC (LLM Serving)
short_definition: A high-performance RPC framework using HTTP/2 and protobuf — common for internal LLM serving where JSON overhead matters.
full_explanation: gRPC offers bidirectional streaming, tight schema enforcement via protobuf, and lower serialization overhead than JSON. Triton Inference Server, NIM, and many internal LLM platforms expose gRPC alongside or instead of HTTP+JSON. For high-QPS internal services with polyglot clients, gRPC is often the right choice; for public APIs, HTTP+JSON wins on accessibility.
why_it_matters: For internal LLM infrastructure at scale, gRPC's efficiency and schemas are real wins over REST.

title: LiteLLM
short_definition: An open-source Python SDK and gateway that wraps 100+ LLM providers behind an OpenAI-compatible interface.
full_explanation: LiteLLM normalizes every major provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Cohere, Mistral, local models via Ollama/vLLM) under the OpenAI Chat Completions API surface. It also ships a server-mode 'LiteLLM Proxy' that adds cost tracking, budgets, key management, and observability. Widely adopted as the default abstraction layer for multi-provider Python codebases.
why_it_matters: LiteLLM is the easiest way to write provider-agnostic code and switch models by string name.

title: Agent Card
short_definition: A JSON document that describes an agent's capabilities, auth, and endpoints in the A2A protocol — discoverable at a well-known URL.
full_explanation: An agent card (served typically at /.well-known/agent-card.json) advertises: the agent's name, supported skills, supported content modalities, auth schemes, A2A endpoint URLs, and streaming support. Clients fetch the card, decide if the agent is suitable, handle auth, then make A2A calls. Think robots.txt meets OpenAPI for agents.
why_it_matters: Agent cards are how A2A-compatible agents discover each other. Useful to know when building A2A-native services.

title: Assistants API (OpenAI)
short_definition: OpenAI's stateful API (predecessor to Responses API) for building assistants with persistent threads, file search, and code interpreter.
full_explanation: Assistants API (2023–2025) introduced threads as a first-class concept: conversations with message history, attached files, and built-in tools (code interpreter, retrieval). Being deprecated in favor of the Responses API but still widely used in code written in 2023–2024. If starting fresh, use Responses; if maintaining existing integrations, understand Assistants.
why_it_matters: Most 'GPT with your data' and 'agent with tools' integrations built 2023–2024 used Assistants. Knowing it matters for legacy maintenance.

title: Embeddings API
short_definition: The API endpoint provided by LLM vendors for converting text (and increasingly images) to vector embeddings for search, classification, and clustering.
full_explanation: Embeddings APIs (OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere Embed v3, Voyage, Google text-embedding) take text input and return dense vectors. Parameters vary: dimension (some support truncation via Matryoshka), input type (query vs. document — some models care), and content modality. Batching is supported; typical limits are thousands of items per request. Core primitive for RAG and semantic search.
why_it_matters: Embeddings APIs are the starting point for any RAG or semantic-search system on managed providers.

title: MCP Sampling
short_definition: An MCP feature that lets a server request LLM completions through the client, so server authors can build LLM-backed workflows without bundling a model.
full_explanation: MCP's sampling capability flips the flow: normally the client uses the LLM and calls server tools. With sampling, the server asks the client (which has LLM access) to generate text. This lets tool servers implement features like 'summarize this log' without needing an API key or model. Clients typically show a permission prompt before responding. Enables privacy-preserving LLM use inside tools.
why_it_matters: Sampling unlocks a new class of MCP server patterns — LLM-using tools without per-tool API keys.

title: Tokenizer
short_definition: The component that converts raw text into integer token IDs (and back) before and after model inference.
full_explanation: Tokenizers sit at the boundary between the natural-language world and the model's numeric world. They split text into subword units using BPE, WordPiece, or Unigram algorithms, map each piece to a vocabulary ID, and add special tokens (BOS, EOS, padding). On output, the reverse happens. Tokenizer choice shapes how many tokens a given text consumes, how well rare characters and languages work, and how clean the chat format is.
why_it_matters: Tokenizer differences explain cost differences between models on the same text, and rare-language failures. Know yours.

title: WordPiece
short_definition: A subword tokenization algorithm (used by BERT) that greedily segments words into pieces that maximize training-corpus likelihood.
full_explanation: WordPiece (Schuster & Nakajima 2012) iteratively merges character pairs that maximize a language-model likelihood objective, producing a vocabulary of subwords. BERT's original tokenizer uses WordPiece with a ## prefix to mark continuations ('play' + '##ing'). Less common in modern LLMs — BPE and Unigram dominate — but BERT-derived models still ship with it.
why_it_matters: Understanding WordPiece helps reason about BERT and its descendants. Legacy but still important for retrieval pipelines.

title: SentencePiece
short_definition: Google's open-source tokenizer library that treats the input as a raw sequence of characters (no word-boundary assumption), supporting both BPE and Unigram LM algorithms.
full_explanation: SentencePiece trains directly on raw text with whitespace encoded as a special character (▁), making it language-agnostic — no pre-tokenization step needed. Supports BPE and Unigram LM training. Used by T5, ALBERT, XLNet, Llama (via a modified version), and most multilingual models. A common choice when languages without whitespace (Chinese, Japanese) are in scope.
why_it_matters: SentencePiece is the tokenizer library behind most multilingual models. Essential when deploying beyond English.

title: Unigram LM Tokenizer
short_definition: A tokenization algorithm that starts with a large seed vocabulary and iteratively prunes it to maximize log-likelihood under a unigram language model.
full_explanation: Unigram LM tokenization (Kudo 2018) is top-down: start with many candidate subwords, fit a unigram language model, drop the pieces whose removal causes the smallest likelihood loss, repeat until target vocab size. Implemented in SentencePiece. Produces multiple valid segmentations of a word — the algorithm picks the highest-probability one — which is different from BPE's deterministic merge-based approach. Used by XLNet and ALBERT.
why_it_matters: Unigram tokenization is the main alternative to BPE. Understanding it is key if you work across both tokenizer families.

title: Vocabulary Size
short_definition: The number of distinct tokens a tokenizer can emit — typically 30k–260k for modern LLMs, trading expressiveness for embedding-table size.
full_explanation: Vocabulary size is a trade-off. Larger vocabs represent text in fewer tokens (cheaper inference, shorter sequences) but require larger embedding tables (more parameters, more memory). BERT used 30k; Llama 2 used 32k; Llama 3 grew to 128k to better handle multilingual text; GPT-4o's o200k_base is ~200k. Embedding and LM-head matrices scale linearly with vocab size.
why_it_matters: Larger vocabs mean fewer tokens per text — sometimes halving inference cost for multilingual workloads.

title: ShareGPT Format
short_definition: A multi-turn conversation dataset format popularized by the ShareGPT dataset, using role-tagged message arrays.
full_explanation: ShareGPT format stores conversations as arrays of {from: 'human'|'gpt', value: '...'} messages. It became the default format for many open-source instruction-tuning datasets (Vicuna, WizardLM, many community fine-tunes) because ShareGPT was the first widely shared dump of real ChatGPT conversations. Training frameworks like Axolotl, LLaMA-Factory, and Unsloth support it natively.
why_it_matters: If you download any 2023–2024 instruction dataset from Hugging Face, it is likely in ShareGPT format. Know the shape.

title: Token Budget
short_definition: The total number of tokens an application allocates to a single LLM call — system prompt + messages + retrieved context + output headroom.
full_explanation: Every call has a hard cap (model's context window) and a soft cap (your cost ceiling). Designing within a token budget means fitting: system prompt (usually small), conversation history (bounded by policy), retrieved context (the flexible lever), and reserved output space. Techniques: truncate history beyond N turns, summarize older turns, compress retrieved chunks, use RAG fusion sparingly.
why_it_matters: Token budget is the economic limit of every LLM feature. Designing to it is more important than picking the right model.

title: Token Cost
short_definition: The dollars-per-million-tokens price charged for inputs and outputs — the unit economics of every LLM feature.
full_explanation: LLM pricing is almost universally per-million-tokens, split between input and output (output is usually 3–5x more expensive). Prompt caching, batch APIs, and open-source self-hosting can change the formula dramatically. Monitoring cost per request, per user, or per feature is table stakes for any serious LLM product. Common traps: exploding retrieval contexts, runaway agent loops, cached-but-paid-for content.
why_it_matters: LLM products live or die by cost per interaction. Tracking it explicitly is how you avoid surprise bills.

title: Prefix Tokens / BOS (Beginning of Sequence)
short_definition: Special tokens (<s>, <|begin_of_text|>, [CLS]) prepended to every sequence to mark where it starts — a sentinel the model uses to anchor attention.
full_explanation: The BOS token (Beginning of Sequence) tells the model where a sequence starts. Different model families use different BOS strings — Llama uses <|begin_of_text|>, BERT uses [CLS], GPT-2 uses no BOS by default. Missing or duplicating BOS at inference when the model expected one during training produces measurable quality drops. Chat templates usually insert BOS automatically.
why_it_matters: A missed BOS is a silent quality bug. Always check how your tokenizer handles sequence start.

title: EOS (End of Sequence) Token
short_definition: A special token that tells the model to stop generating — hitting it during inference is how the model signals 'I'm done.'
full_explanation: The EOS token (</s>, <|eot_id|>, <|end_of_text|>) tells inference engines to halt decoding. Training cross-entropy includes EOS as a target, so the model learns when to emit it. Misconfigured EOS handling (wrong string, not registered as stop token) causes models to generate past where they should have stopped. Chat templates typically insert per-turn EOS tokens.
why_it_matters: EOS misconfiguration is a top source of 'the model keeps going forever' bugs. Always verify your stop token list.

title: Pad Token
short_definition: A special token used to pad short sequences in a batch to a common length so they can be processed together efficiently.
full_explanation: Padding lets GPUs process varying-length sequences in a single batched tensor. Pad tokens are masked out of the loss during training and ignored by attention via the attention mask. Decoder-only models with left padding (pad on the left so actual content ends at the same position) are standard for generation. Some models have no dedicated pad token — use EOS or a reserved unused token.
why_it_matters: Incorrect padding — wrong side, unmasked, or shared with EOS — is a common source of silent training bugs.

title: Tokenizer Vocabulary Extension
short_definition: Adding new tokens to an existing tokenizer — for languages, domain jargon, or special structural tokens — without retraining from scratch.
full_explanation: You can extend a tokenizer vocabulary by appending tokens and resizing the model's embedding matrix. New tokens start with random embeddings that need fine-tuning to be meaningful. Use cases: extending to a new language, adding domain terms ('GeneX1') that otherwise split into many tokens, or introducing structural tokens like <reasoning> for reasoning models. Requires retraining the LM head and ideally the embedding layer.
why_it_matters: Vocab extension is the standard path for language-specific adaptation without full retraining.

title: vLLM
short_definition: A high-throughput LLM inference and serving engine from UC Berkeley, centered on PagedAttention for efficient KV cache management.
full_explanation: vLLM uses PagedAttention to store KV cache in fixed-size blocks like virtual memory pages, enabling memory sharing across requests with common prefixes and eliminating fragmentation. Combined with continuous batching, this delivers 5–20x throughput over naive Transformers inference. Supports most popular models, OpenAI-compatible API, tensor parallelism, quantization (AWQ, GPTQ, FP8), LoRA adapters, and speculative decoding. The default serving stack for self-hosted LLMs.
why_it_matters: vLLM is the most-deployed open-source LLM inference engine. If you are serving LLMs on your own GPUs, this is where you start.

title: RadixAttention
short_definition: SGLang's KV cache data structure — a radix tree that automatically discovers and reuses shared prompt prefixes across concurrent requests.
full_explanation: RadixAttention (Zheng et al. 2023) stores the KV cache as nodes of a radix tree keyed by token IDs. When a new prompt arrives, it walks down the tree to find the longest matching prefix, reusing that cache rather than recomputing. Automatically catches shared system prompts, few-shot examples, and conversation prefixes without application-level coordination. LRU eviction handles memory pressure.
why_it_matters: RadixAttention provides prefix-caching benefits automatically, without the application having to mark cached regions.

title: TensorRT-LLM
short_definition: Nvidia's optimized LLM inference library that compiles model graphs to TensorRT with custom kernels for maximum H100/B200 throughput.
full_explanation: TensorRT-LLM takes a PyTorch model, traces and compiles it into TensorRT graph format with fused CUDA kernels, FP8 support on Hopper/Blackwell, and speculative decoding. Typically the fastest inference option on Nvidia hardware when tuned, but requires more engineering than vLLM. Supports in-flight batching, tensor parallelism, and most current model architectures. The backend behind Nvidia's Triton LLM engine and many managed inference services.
why_it_matters: For absolute top-end performance on Nvidia GPUs, TensorRT-LLM is the reference stack.

title: Ollama
short_definition: A developer-friendly runtime for running LLMs locally via simple CLI/REST commands, wrapping llama.cpp with a curated model library.
full_explanation: Ollama gives you 'docker-like' UX for LLMs: 'ollama run llama3.2' downloads and runs the model with defaults tuned for your hardware. It exposes an OpenAI-compatible API at localhost:11434 and handles GPU/CPU offload automatically. Model library includes Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Phi, and thousands of community-provided fine-tunes. A popular default for local development and hobbyist deployments.
why_it_matters: Ollama removed the friction of local LLMs. For development workflows, it is the fastest path to a running model.

title: LM Studio
short_definition: A cross-platform desktop app for running LLMs locally with a GUI and OpenAI-compatible local server — no command line needed.
full_explanation: LM Studio (Mac, Windows, Linux) lets non-technical users download GGUF models from Hugging Face, chat in a GUI, and start a local OpenAI-compatible server with a click. Built on llama.cpp. Popular with analysts, lawyers, and others who want local AI without setting up Python environments. Supports Apple Silicon MPS, CUDA, ROCm, and Vulkan backends.
why_it_matters: LM Studio is the most accessible way to run local LLMs. Great for privacy-sensitive personal use.

title: llama.cpp (server)
short_definition: The inference server built into the llama.cpp project — a lightweight C/C++ HTTP server that runs GGUF models with minimal dependencies.
full_explanation: The llama.cpp server binary exposes a local REST API (and OpenAI-compatible endpoint) backed by the llama.cpp inference engine. Supports GGUF quantized models (Q4_K_M, Q5_K_S, Q8_0), Metal/CUDA/Vulkan acceleration, LoRA hot-loading, and multimodal (LLaVA-style). Often the right choice when you want something lighter than vLLM or need to run on edge hardware. Ollama and LM Studio both use it as their backend.
why_it_matters: llama.cpp is the CPU/edge backbone of open-source local AI. Understanding it matters whenever Ollama or LM Studio don't fit.

title: MLC-LLM
short_definition: A universal LLM deployment framework that compiles models for any device — iOS, Android, WebGPU, CUDA, Metal, Vulkan — from a single codebase.
full_explanation: MLC-LLM (Machine Learning Compilation) uses Apache TVM under the hood to compile LLMs into platform-specific code. You can run Llama on an iPhone via Metal, on Android via Vulkan, in a browser via WebGPU, and on a server via CUDA — same model, different compiled kernels. Well-suited to teams targeting multiple platforms without rewriting inference code each time.
why_it_matters: MLC-LLM is the most cross-platform LLM runtime. For mobile and edge deployment with consistent tooling, it is a top choice.

title: ExLlama (ExLlamaV2)
short_definition: A fast GPU-only inference library for quantized Llama-family models, popular for consumer-GPU deployments due to superior speed on GPTQ/EXL2 models.
full_explanation: ExLlamaV2 is optimized for consumer Nvidia GPUs running 2–8-bit quantized models. Its EXL2 format supports mixed-precision bit-widths per layer for fine-grained quality/size trade-offs. Integrates with Text Generation WebUI, TabbyAPI, and KoboldCpp. A community favorite for RP and local chat where you want maximum throughput on a single 3090/4090.
why_it_matters: For the best speed on a single consumer GPU running quantized models, ExLlamaV2 often wins.

title: Continuous Batching (Inflight Batching)
short_definition: LLM serving optimization where new requests join an in-progress batch at each decoding step, instead of waiting for the batch to complete.
full_explanation: Traditional static batching waits for a full batch before starting decoding, then all sequences progress until everyone finishes — slowest sequence sets the pace. Continuous batching (Yu et al. 2022, implemented in vLLM, TGI, TensorRT-LLM) admits new requests each decode step, evicting finished ones. Result: much higher GPU utilization (2–5x throughput) with no latency penalty for short requests. The standard modern serving pattern.
why_it_matters: Continuous batching is why modern serving is fast. Without it, utilization on mixed-workload traffic tanks.

title: Dynamic Batching
short_definition: A batching strategy where requests are grouped on the fly based on a time window or max batch size rather than waiting for a pre-defined batch.
full_explanation: Dynamic batching (common in Triton, BentoML, Ray Serve) collects incoming requests in a short window (e.g., 10 ms), then launches a batched inference call. Parameters: max_batch_size, max_queue_delay. Simpler than continuous batching but less efficient for long-running LLM decoding; still the default for short-lived inference workloads like embeddings, classification, and image models.
why_it_matters: Dynamic batching is the right choice for short-inference models. For LLM text generation, use continuous batching instead.

title: Prefix Caching (Inference)
short_definition: Reusing the KV cache of shared prompt prefixes across requests to skip recomputation — the inference-engine counterpart to API-level prompt caching.
full_explanation: Prefix caching detects that incoming prompts share prefixes (system prompt, few-shot examples, long conversation history) and reuses the already-computed KV cache for that shared portion. Implementations: vLLM PagedAttention's copy-on-write, SGLang RadixAttention, TensorRT-LLM enable_kv_cache_reuse. Savings scale with prefix length — for a 2000-token system prompt reused across many requests, the first forward pass for that prefix is paid once, not N times.
why_it_matters: Prefix caching is the biggest automatic cost/latency win in LLM serving. Always enable it if your engine supports it.

title: Prefill
short_definition: The first phase of LLM inference where the entire prompt is processed in parallel to initialize the KV cache — compute-bound and fast on GPUs.
full_explanation: LLM inference has two phases: prefill (process the prompt) and decode (generate output token by token). Prefill is parallel — all input tokens run through every transformer layer at once, producing the full KV cache. This phase is compute-bound and saturates GPU FLOPs. With a 1000-token prompt, prefill is a single big matmul; decode is 1000 sequential tiny matmuls. This asymmetry drives most inference optimization trade-offs.
why_it_matters: Understanding prefill vs. decode is foundational to reasoning about LLM serving cost and latency.

title: Decode (Generation Phase)
short_definition: The LLM inference phase where tokens are generated one at a time, each pass reusing the KV cache — memory-bandwidth bound, not compute-bound.
full_explanation: Decode runs one forward pass per generated token, attending to the accumulated KV cache. Each pass is a tiny matmul (single-row weight × vector) that barely uses the GPU's compute; total time is dominated by reading model weights from HBM to SMs. This is why quantization — shrinking weights — directly speeds up decode. Batching multiple sequences amortizes weight reads across concurrent generations.
why_it_matters: Decode is where time and money go in LLM inference. Optimizations (quantization, batching, speculative decoding) target this phase.

title: Chunked Prefill
short_definition: An inference scheduling technique that splits long prefills into chunks so decoding tokens from other requests can interleave, reducing tail latency.
full_explanation: A large prefill (e.g., a 30k-token RAG context) blocks the GPU for a noticeable time, delaying every other request's next decode step. Chunked prefill (vLLM, SGLang, Triton) splits the prefill into small chunks — say, 512 tokens at a time — and interleaves them with decode work from other sessions. Result: much better tail latency for mixed workloads without hurting throughput.
why_it_matters: Chunked prefill is essential for multi-tenant serving where long-context requests mix with short chat requests.

title: RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding)
short_definition: A position encoding that rotates query and key vectors by position-dependent angles, enabling relative position awareness and straightforward context-length extension.
full_explanation: RoPE (Su et al. 2021) encodes position by applying rotation matrices to Q and K vectors in attention, where rotation angle depends on position and feature dimension. Unlike absolute embeddings, dot products encode relative position. Used by Llama, Qwen, Mistral, GPT-NeoX, and most 2023+ models. Extensions like YaRN and NTK scaling enable post-hoc context-length extension by interpolating or extrapolating the rotation frequencies.
why_it_matters: RoPE is the dominant position encoding in modern LLMs. Understanding it is key to context-length extensions.

title: RoPE Scaling / YaRN
short_definition: Techniques for extending a RoPE-based model's context window beyond its training length by reshaping the rotation frequencies.
full_explanation: A model trained at 4k tokens degrades quickly past that length because RoPE frequencies were not seen beyond training. Scaling methods: Linear scaling (divide position index), NTK-aware scaling (rescale base frequency), Dynamic NTK (adjust at inference based on input length), YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN — per-dimension interpolation + temperature rescaling, currently SOTA). A small amount of fine-tuning after scaling usually recovers full quality at the new length.
why_it_matters: RoPE scaling is how 4k-trained Llamas became 128k-context models. A routine ops technique.

title: ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases)
short_definition: A position-encoding alternative that adds a linear bias to attention scores based on query-key distance, enabling strong extrapolation to unseen sequence lengths.
full_explanation: ALiBi (Press et al. 2021) replaces explicit position embeddings with a simple distance-penalty added to attention scores. The penalty increases linearly with distance, with a per-head slope. ALiBi models extrapolate better to longer sequences than sinusoidal or learned position embeddings. Used by MPT, BLOOM, and some research models. Less common now that RoPE dominates, but still a reasonable choice for long-context.
why_it_matters: ALiBi is worth knowing when reading papers from 2022–2023 and for teams exploring extrapolation-friendly architectures.

title: GQA (Grouped Query Attention)
short_definition: An attention variant where multiple query heads share the same key/value heads, reducing KV cache size and memory bandwidth without much quality loss.
full_explanation: Multi-head attention has N heads each with their own Q, K, V projections. GQA (Ainslie et al. 2023) partitions N query heads into G groups, each group sharing one set of K, V projections. Typical choice: 8 query heads grouped to 1 KV head (so 8x KV cache reduction). Used by Llama 2 70B, Llama 3, and most modern LLMs. Multi-Query Attention (MQA) is the extreme case where all heads share one K, V head.
why_it_matters: GQA is standard in modern LLMs for a reason — free memory savings with negligible quality impact.

title: MQA (Multi-Query Attention)
short_definition: An attention variant where all query heads share a single key/value head pair, maximally reducing KV cache size.
full_explanation: MQA (Shazeer 2019) takes GQA to the limit — one K head and one V head for all queries. KV cache is tiny (roughly 1/N of standard multi-head), enabling larger batch sizes and longer contexts on the same hardware. Quality impact is more noticeable than GQA, so most modern models use GQA as a compromise rather than MQA. PaLM and early inference research adopted MQA; production models now usually choose GQA.
why_it_matters: MQA set the stage for GQA. Still a valid choice when memory is the hardest constraint.

title: Sliding Window Attention
short_definition: An attention pattern where each token attends only to the last K tokens rather than the full sequence — linear-cost attention at the price of limited long-range context.
full_explanation: Sliding window attention (Longformer, Mistral) truncates attention to a fixed window of recent tokens (e.g., 4096). Cost becomes linear in sequence length rather than quadratic. Models usually interleave sliding-window layers with a few full-attention layers so information can still propagate across long distances. Mistral 7B uses sliding window with W=4096 combined with a 32k-token nominal context.
why_it_matters: Sliding window is one way to get long-context inference without quadratic cost. Understanding trade-offs is key when choosing long-context strategies.

title: RMSNorm
short_definition: Root Mean Square Normalization — a normalization layer that skips the mean-centering of LayerNorm, saving compute with equivalent quality.
full_explanation: LayerNorm normalizes by subtracting the mean and dividing by the std. RMSNorm (Zhang & Sennrich 2019) drops the mean subtraction, normalizing only by the RMS of the activations. Equivalent quality on language modeling with less compute — one fewer reduction, one fewer broadcast. Every modern LLM (Llama, Mistral, Gemma, Qwen) uses RMSNorm in place of LayerNorm.
why_it_matters: RMSNorm is a small architectural choice with modest but real gains. It is now the standard in decoder-only LLMs.

title: SwiGLU Activation
short_definition: A gated linear unit activation that combines Swish and GLU, used in most modern LLM feed-forward layers for better quality than plain ReLU/GELU.
full_explanation: SwiGLU (Shazeer 2020) replaces the FFN activation with SwiGLU(x) = Swish(xW) ⊗ (xV), where W and V are separate linear projections and ⊗ is element-wise multiply. Introduces a gating mechanism similar to LSTM gates. Requires ~1.5x more parameters in the FFN than ReLU/GELU but consistently delivers lower loss at the same training budget. Used by Llama, PaLM, and every major modern LLM.
why_it_matters: SwiGLU is one of the quiet architectural choices behind modern LLM quality. Mostly invisible to users but ubiquitous in SOTA models.

title: GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)
short_definition: Massively parallel processors with thousands of cores optimized for matrix math — the workhorse of nearly all ML training and inference.
full_explanation: GPUs pack thousands of simple cores that run the same instruction across many data points (SIMT). This maps perfectly onto the dense matrix multiplications in neural networks. Nvidia dominates (CUDA ecosystem), AMD is catching up (ROCm), and specialized accelerators (TPUs, Trainium, Inferentia, Gaudi) are niche. For LLMs, GPU choice shapes training budget and inference economics more than any other decision.
why_it_matters: Which GPU you have determines what models you can run and how fast. The most consequential hardware decision in any ML project.

title: H100 (Nvidia Hopper)
short_definition: Nvidia's Hopper-architecture data-center GPU — the workhorse of frontier LLM training and inference from 2023–2024.
full_explanation: H100 (80GB HBM3, ~3 TB/s bandwidth, ~989 TFLOPs bf16) replaced A100 as the dominant training GPU. Introduced FP8 Tensor Cores, Transformer Engine hardware, and NVLink 4.0 for 900 GB/s GPU-to-GPU interconnect. Most frontier models (GPT-4-era, Claude 3, Llama 3) trained on H100 clusters. Still the most-deployed data-center GPU in late 2025, though B200 (Blackwell) is taking over new builds.
why_it_matters: If you are buying or renting training compute in 2024–2026, H100 is likely what you are getting.

title: A100 (Nvidia Ampere)
short_definition: Nvidia's Ampere-architecture data-center GPU that powered most of the pre-H100 LLM era — GPT-3, early Llama, and most 2020–2022 research.
full_explanation: A100 (40GB or 80GB HBM2e, ~1.5–2 TB/s bandwidth, ~312 TFLOPs bf16) dominated 2020–2023. It introduced structured 2:4 sparsity, multi-instance GPU partitioning, and strong bf16 support that made mixed-precision training standard. Now considered mid-tier for training frontier models but still widely used for inference, fine-tuning, and academic research thanks to secondary-market availability.
why_it_matters: A100 remains the price-performance sweet spot for many inference and fine-tuning workloads. Still widely deployed.

title: B200 (Nvidia Blackwell)
short_definition: Nvidia's Blackwell-architecture GPU (2024+), doubling H100 performance with FP4 support and larger HBM3e memory — the new frontier training platform.
full_explanation: B200 (192GB HBM3e, ~8 TB/s bandwidth, ~20 PFLOPs FP4 / ~5 PFLOPs FP8) effectively doubles H100's throughput. GB200 pairs two B200 GPUs with a Grace CPU. NVLink 5.0 brings intra-node bandwidth to 1.8 TB/s. FP4 support enables even-lower-precision training and inference research. Frontier labs were transitioning to Blackwell through 2024–2026.
why_it_matters: B200 is the current frontier. Training budgets at top labs assume it.

title: L4 / L40S (Inference GPUs)
short_definition: Nvidia's inference-optimized Ada Lovelace GPUs — L4 (24GB, edge/dev) and L40S (48GB, server) — lower TDP alternatives to H100 for serving.
full_explanation: L4 and L40S focus on inference throughput per dollar rather than training throughput. L40S has 48GB GDDR6, 864 GB/s bandwidth, strong FP8 support, and TDP around 350W versus H100's 700W. For inference of models that fit in 48GB (up to ~30B dense at bf16 or 70B+ with quantization) L40S often wins on cost efficiency. Popular with mid-tier cloud providers (Lambda, Runpod, CoreWeave) for managed inference.
why_it_matters: For production inference, L40S and L4 often beat H100 on cost-per-token. Worth considering when training is not the concern.

title: GH200 (Nvidia Grace Hopper)
short_definition: Nvidia's coherent-memory superchip pairing a Grace CPU with an H100 GPU via 900 GB/s NVLink, useful for massive-context LLMs and large model inference.
full_explanation: GH200 combines a 72-core ARM-based Grace CPU with an H100 GPU and 480+144 GB of coherent unified memory. The CPU and GPU share address space at full NVLink bandwidth, so CPU offloading of KV cache or expert weights becomes viable without the usual PCIe bottleneck. Particularly useful for very long-context serving and large MoE inference where not all weights fit in HBM.
why_it_matters: GH200 unlocks inference patterns (seamless CPU offload) that PCIe-connected GPUs cannot match.

title: MI300X (AMD Instinct)
short_definition: AMD's data-center GPU with 192GB HBM3 and strong bf16 performance — a credible non-Nvidia option for LLM inference and some training.
full_explanation: MI300X (late 2023) offers 192GB HBM3 — the largest memory of any single GPU at launch, roughly 2.4x H100. Memory bandwidth ~5.3 TB/s. Bf16 and fp8 compute competitive with H100. Uses ROCm software stack, with support for PyTorch, vLLM, Triton, and most HF tooling. Adoption is growing at cloud providers looking for Nvidia alternatives. Software maturity lags CUDA but gap is narrowing fast.
why_it_matters: MI300X is the first AMD GPU to credibly challenge H100 in production LLM workloads. Worth tracking as a cost alternative.

title: TPU (Tensor Processing Unit)
short_definition: Google's custom ML accelerator — TPU v4, v5, v6 — optimized for massive matmul throughput via systolic arrays, used to train Gemini and Gemma.
full_explanation: TPUs are Google-designed ASICs built around large systolic arrays that maximize matmul density. Available via Google Cloud (TPU VM, Cloud TPU Pod). TPU v5p pods reach many exaflops of bf16. JAX is the native programming model, though PyTorch/XLA works. TPUs are the backbone of Google's internal training (Gemini, Gemma) and compete with Nvidia on training economics in Google's cloud.
why_it_matters: For large-scale training on Google Cloud, TPUs are often more cost-effective than Nvidia. The main non-Nvidia option for training.

title: AWS Trainium / Inferentia
short_definition: Amazon's custom training (Trainium) and inference (Inferentia) accelerators, accessed via AWS Neuron SDK as a lower-cost alternative to Nvidia on AWS.
full_explanation: Trainium and Inferentia chips are Amazon's answer to building a vertically integrated ML stack. Trainium2 (2024) claims H100-class performance at lower price, targeting training of 100B+ models on Trn2 UltraClusters. Inferentia2 targets inference cost optimization. Programmed via AWS Neuron SDK (a PyTorch compiler). Adoption is growing but tooling maturity lags Nvidia. Anthropic has publicly built on Trainium.
why_it_matters: For AWS-committed shops, Trainium/Inferentia can significantly cut training/inference bills at the cost of ecosystem breadth.

title: CUDA
short_definition: Nvidia's parallel-compute platform and programming model that nearly every ML framework targets, creating the moat around Nvidia GPUs.
full_explanation: CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture, 2007) lets developers write C/C++ code that runs on Nvidia GPUs. Most ML frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) call CUDA libraries (cuBLAS, cuDNN, cuDNN, cuFFT) under the hood. CUDA's 15+ years of tooling, libraries, and community are why Nvidia remains dominant even when competitors have comparable hardware. Alternatives: ROCm (AMD), oneAPI (Intel), Metal (Apple), OpenCL (generic).
why_it_matters: CUDA is the de facto ML compute standard. Hardware alternatives live or die by how well they emulate or compile to it.

title: ROCm (AMD)
short_definition: AMD's open-source alternative to CUDA — compute platform, runtime, and libraries for running ML workloads on AMD Instinct GPUs.
full_explanation: ROCm (Radeon Open Compute, 2016+) is AMD's counterpart to CUDA. Includes HIP (a CUDA-like C++ API), MIOpen (cuDNN analogue), rocBLAS, and a PyTorch/TensorFlow backend. Historically lagged CUDA in stability and performance, but major recent investment (spurred by MI300X) has closed much of the gap. PyTorch 2+ with ROCm works for most LLM training and inference workloads.
why_it_matters: For AMD GPU deployments, ROCm is the stack. Maturity matters when evaluating AMD as a Nvidia alternative.

title: Triton (Language / Compiler)
short_definition: OpenAI's open-source Python-like DSL for writing GPU kernels that often match hand-tuned CUDA performance with far less code.
full_explanation: Triton (Tillet et al. 2019, OpenAI) compiles Python-like kernel code into efficient GPU code via its own MLIR-based compiler. It handles memory coalescing, shared memory, and scheduling automatically. Used to implement FlashAttention, Liger kernels, and many state-of-the-art LLM inference kernels. Also the IR backend for PyTorch 2's torch.compile on NVIDIA GPUs. Confusingly named the same as Nvidia's Triton Inference Server — they are unrelated.
why_it_matters: Triton is how modern performance-sensitive ML kernels get written. Reading Triton code is increasingly a core skill for performance work.

title: Triton Inference Server (Nvidia)
short_definition: Nvidia's open-source inference serving framework supporting multiple backends (TensorRT, PyTorch, ONNX, Python, TensorRT-LLM) with HTTP/gRPC endpoints.
full_explanation: Triton Inference Server (unrelated to OpenAI's Triton language) hosts trained models behind a uniform API. Backends: TensorRT for peak throughput, TensorRT-LLM for LLMs, PyTorch/ONNX for flexibility, Python for custom logic. Features: dynamic batching, ensemble models (chaining multiple models), multi-model GPU sharing, Prometheus metrics, and model-repository hot-reload. Common in high-throughput production inference on Nvidia hardware.
why_it_matters: Triton Server is the default serving platform for mixed-model production ML on Nvidia hardware.

title: Safetensors
short_definition: A secure file format for storing model weights — fast, memory-mappable, and immune to the arbitrary code execution vulnerabilities in pickle-based formats.
full_explanation: Safetensors (Hugging Face, 2022) stores tensor weights as a flat binary blob prefixed by a JSON header describing each tensor's shape, dtype, and offset. Memory-mapped loading is zero-copy fast, and critically the format cannot execute arbitrary code on load — unlike .bin/.pt files which use pickle. Became the default for Hugging Face model distribution in 2023. Models you download in 2025 are overwhelmingly safetensors.
why_it_matters: Safetensors is the safe default for sharing models. Using pickle-based formats from untrusted sources is a real security risk.

title: ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange)
short_definition: An open format for representing ML models so they can move between frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) and runtimes (ONNX Runtime, TensorRT).
full_explanation: ONNX (2017) defines a standard graph format with typed tensors and operators. Export a PyTorch model to ONNX, then run it on ONNX Runtime (cross-platform C++), TensorRT (Nvidia optimized), or many edge runtimes. ONNX is well-suited to classical CNNs and smaller models; very large LLMs push ONNX's limits, so most LLM serving uses PyTorch or framework-native tools. Still the dominant format for edge and cross-platform inference.
why_it_matters: For mobile/edge ML deployment, ONNX is the most common interchange format. Worth knowing.

title: ONNX Runtime
short_definition: Microsoft's cross-platform ML inference engine that runs ONNX models efficiently on CPUs, GPUs, mobile devices, and browsers.
full_explanation: ONNX Runtime supports execution providers — pluggable backends for CUDA, DirectML, CoreML, TensorRT, OpenVINO, and WebGPU. One ONNX model, many runtimes. Particularly strong on Windows (first-party Microsoft tooling), for cross-platform desktop apps, and for edge inference. ONNX Runtime Web runs ONNX models in the browser via WebAssembly and WebGPU, enabling in-browser LLM inference for small models.
why_it_matters: ONNX Runtime is the cross-platform inference option. First choice for 'run this model everywhere.'

title: OpenVINO (Intel)
short_definition: Intel's inference toolkit for deploying ML models on Intel CPUs, iGPUs, dGPUs, and specialized accelerators — strong on CPU inference optimization.
full_explanation: OpenVINO compiles models to an Intel-optimized intermediate representation and runs them via its inference engine. Excellent CPU inference performance — often the fastest CPU-only runtime for classical CNNs and smaller LLMs. Supports Intel Arc and Data Center GPU Max series. Integration with Hugging Face via the Optimum library lets you export Transformers models to OpenVINO with a command.
why_it_matters: For CPU-only inference or Intel-GPU deployments, OpenVINO is often the fastest option.

title: CoreML (Apple)
short_definition: Apple's on-device ML framework for iOS, macOS, and visionOS — running models on Apple Neural Engine, GPU, or CPU depending on what fits best.
full_explanation: Core ML (2017) lets Apple apps run ML models with automatic backend selection between CPU, GPU (Metal Performance Shaders), and Apple Neural Engine (ANE, a specialized NPU). Core ML Tools convert PyTorch and TensorFlow models to .mlpackage. Apple's Private Cloud Compute and Apple Intelligence (2024) are built on Core ML. Small LLMs (7B class, quantized) run well on M-series Macs via Core ML or MLX.
why_it_matters: For iOS/macOS apps, Core ML is the on-device ML platform. MLX is the research/training complement on Apple Silicon.

title: MLX (Apple)
short_definition: Apple's array framework for Apple Silicon — a PyTorch-like API with lazy evaluation optimized for unified memory architecture on M-series chips.
full_explanation: MLX (Dec 2023) lets researchers train and run models efficiently on MacBooks and Mac Studios. The unified memory model means CPU and GPU share the same address space — no copying across a PCIe bus, which is transformative for large-model work on limited hardware. mlx_lm runs popular LLMs including Llama, Qwen, Mistral. Used by the Apple Research team and increasingly by developers for on-device experimentation.
why_it_matters: MLX turns M-series Macs into capable local LLM research machines. For Apple-hardware developers, it is the native tool.

title: WebGPU
short_definition: A modern browser graphics/compute API (successor to WebGL) that enables GPU-accelerated ML inference in the browser without plugins.
full_explanation: WebGPU (W3C, stable in Chrome 2023, Safari/Firefox 2024–2025) exposes modern GPU compute to web apps. Libraries like Transformers.js (Hugging Face), MLC-LLM WebLLM, and ONNX Runtime Web use WebGPU to run 1B–7B parameter models entirely in the browser, local to the user's machine. Enables privacy-preserving in-browser LLM UX, offline-capable AI features, and free inference.
why_it_matters: WebGPU is the bridge between open-source LLMs and browser apps without server inference costs. A rapidly growing deployment target.

title: Transformers.js
short_definition: Hugging Face's JavaScript port of Transformers, letting models run in Node.js or the browser via ONNX Runtime Web (CPU) or WebGPU (GPU).
full_explanation: Transformers.js (Xenova, 2023) mirrors the Python Transformers API in JavaScript: pipelines for translation, summarization, classification, embeddings, speech, and text generation. Models are ONNX-exported and run via onnxruntime-web. Version 3 (2024) adds WebGPU for significant GPU acceleration in browsers. Popular for in-browser LLM demos, VS Code extensions, and Chrome extensions that do client-side AI.
why_it_matters: Transformers.js is the easiest path to in-browser AI. Powers most 'run an LLM in your browser' demos.

title: NVLink
short_definition: Nvidia's high-bandwidth, low-latency GPU-to-GPU and CPU-to-GPU interconnect, enabling efficient multi-GPU training and inference.
full_explanation: NVLink provides much higher bandwidth than PCIe between GPUs within a node. NVLink 3.0 (A100): 600 GB/s. NVLink 4.0 (H100): 900 GB/s. NVLink 5.0 (B200): 1.8 TB/s. Critical for tensor parallelism, which moves large activation tensors between GPUs during each forward/backward pass. Multi-GPU systems with NVLink are fundamentally different performance-tier than PCIe-only setups.
why_it_matters: NVLink presence determines whether tensor parallelism is viable. A key spec for training-class machines.

title: InfiniBand
short_definition: A high-performance networking standard used between servers in GPU clusters to connect nodes with low-latency, high-bandwidth RDMA.
full_explanation: InfiniBand (Mellanox, now Nvidia) provides 400–800 Gbps links with native RDMA, used to connect nodes in GPU clusters. Essential for inter-node collective operations (all-reduce, all-gather) in distributed training. NCCL uses InfiniBand when available, falling back to Ethernet + RoCE (RDMA over Ethernet). Training large LLMs across many nodes requires InfiniBand or equivalent; plain Ethernet is too slow.
why_it_matters: Training across multiple nodes requires InfiniBand (or equivalent). Cloud GPU instances with Ethernet-only networking cannot match.

title: NCCL (Nvidia Collective Communications Library)
short_definition: Nvidia's optimized library for multi-GPU and multi-node collective operations — all-reduce, all-gather, broadcast — the backbone of distributed training.
full_explanation: NCCL implements efficient ring and tree algorithms for GPU collective communication over NVLink, PCIe, and InfiniBand. PyTorch's DDP, FSDP, and DeepSpeed all use NCCL. Performance is critical — poor NCCL configuration can bottleneck training on otherwise-fast hardware. Tuning includes NCCL_DEBUG logs, NCCL_IB_HCA for InfiniBand routing, and choosing between ring and tree algorithms.
why_it_matters: Every distributed training job runs on NCCL. When training scales poorly, NCCL tuning is often the fix.

title: HBM (High Bandwidth Memory)
short_definition: Stacked DRAM integrated with a GPU or accelerator via a silicon interposer, delivering much higher bandwidth than GDDR memory.
full_explanation: HBM (HBM2, HBM3, HBM3e) uses vertical DRAM stacks with thousands of I/O pins connected via a silicon interposer. Bandwidth per chip: HBM2 ~307 GB/s, HBM3 ~819 GB/s, HBM3e ~1.2 TB/s. Packaged into GPUs (H100 has 5 stacks = ~3 TB/s total, B200 8 stacks = ~8 TB/s). HBM is the reason data-center GPUs cost what they do — and the primary supply-chain bottleneck limiting GPU production.
why_it_matters: HBM bandwidth is the hard limit on LLM decode throughput. Knowing GPU HBM specs directly predicts inference performance.

title: CPU Offloading
short_definition: A memory-saving technique that stores some model weights or optimizer state in CPU RAM and streams them to the GPU as needed.
full_explanation: CPU offloading trades latency for VRAM. DeepSpeed ZeRO-Infinity, FSDP CPU-offload, and bitsandbytes all support it. Parameters, gradients, or optimizer state live in CPU memory; they are fetched over PCIe for each forward/backward. Great for fitting huge models on modest hardware; bad for throughput (PCIe is slow). Use when you have no alternative, not as a default.
why_it_matters: CPU offloading is the 'last resort' lever that lets you train/run models that physically would not fit on your GPUs.

title: Disk Offloading (NVMe Offload)
short_definition: Extending offloading beyond CPU RAM to NVMe SSDs, enabling training of trillion-parameter models on modest clusters at major throughput cost.
full_explanation: DeepSpeed ZeRO-Infinity supports NVMe offload: optimizer state (and with care, parameters and gradients) page to NVMe. Bandwidth: NVMe ~7 GB/s per drive, PCIe 5.0 ~64 GB/s — orders of magnitude slower than HBM. Practical only when you need to squeeze a model in that otherwise would not fit and can tolerate 10–100x slowdowns. Rarely used for frontier training but common in hobbyist fine-tuning of large MoE models.
why_it_matters: NVMe offload makes otherwise-impossible fine-tunes run. It is slow but beats 'cannot run at all.'

title: Flash Attention 2 / 3
short_definition: Successive versions of Flash Attention that improve GPU occupancy, reduce non-matmul overhead, and add support for new precisions and hardware.
full_explanation: FA2 (Tri Dao, 2023) improves parallelism and reduces non-matmul FLOPs, roughly doubling throughput over FA1 on most sequence lengths. FA3 (2024) targets H100-specific features — async copies, TMA (Tensor Memory Accelerator), and FP8 support — delivering another 1.5–2x. Drop-in replacements in most frameworks. FA3 requires specific H100-era hardware and CUDA versions.
why_it_matters: Upgrading from FA1 to FA2/FA3 is often the single biggest LLM training speedup with zero code changes.

title: Liger Kernels
short_definition: LinkedIn's open-source Triton-based kernels for LLM training that deliver significant speedups and memory savings over stock PyTorch implementations.
full_explanation: Liger (2024) fuses common LLM operations — RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE, cross-entropy, FusedLinearCrossEntropy — into single Triton kernels. The fused cross-entropy kernel alone can cut peak training VRAM by 60% for large-vocab models since it skips materializing the full logit tensor. Integrates with Hugging Face Trainer, Axolotl, and Unsloth. Typical 20–40% throughput improvement and memory savings that enable bigger batch sizes.
why_it_matters: Liger is one of the easiest optimizations to enable and gives non-trivial wins. Increasingly default in open-source training stacks.

title: torch.compile
short_definition: PyTorch 2.0's JIT compiler that traces Python code into a graph and compiles it via Inductor (Triton backend) for significant speedups.
full_explanation: torch.compile wraps any callable and, on first invocation, traces the operations into a graph, optimizes it (operator fusion, memory layout), and emits Triton kernels on GPU. Speedups of 20–200% depending on workload. For LLMs, specific attention implementations and kernel choices matter — torch.compile's composition with Flash Attention is now mature. Widely used in research; less so in production where graph-break sensitivity can cause issues.
why_it_matters: torch.compile is the easiest PyTorch-native speedup available. Try it; the gains are often meaningful.

title: JAX
short_definition: Google's array framework combining NumPy-like API with JIT compilation (XLA), automatic differentiation, and pmap/pjit for scaling to TPU pods.
full_explanation: JAX (Google, 2018) is PyTorch's main research-framework alternative. Its functional programming model (pure functions, no state) makes parallelism cleaner; jit/vmap/pmap/pjit compose for easy scaling. XLA backend produces highly optimized code for CPUs, GPUs, and TPUs. Flax and Haiku are the common neural-network libraries on top of JAX. Preferred at Google DeepMind and Anthropic for research; PyTorch dominates industry more broadly.
why_it_matters: JAX is the main non-PyTorch research ecosystem. Any serious ML engineer should know it exists and why to pick it.

title: PyTorch
short_definition: The most widely used deep-learning framework — eager-mode Python APIs with autograd, GPU acceleration, and a vast ecosystem of libraries and models.
full_explanation: PyTorch (Meta, 2016) won the research framework war with its Pythonic API and dynamic computation graphs. PyTorch 2 (2023) added torch.compile and moved closer to TensorFlow's graph-mode strengths without giving up eager flexibility. Every major model — Llama, BERT, Stable Diffusion — ships with reference PyTorch code. Backing by Hugging Face Transformers, Lightning, Accelerate, and vLLM cements its central position.
why_it_matters: PyTorch is the default framework for virtually all deep learning work outside Google. If you only learn one framework, learn this.

title: Hugging Face Transformers
short_definition: The dominant library for loading, running, and fine-tuning pretrained transformer models — BERT, Llama, Stable Diffusion, Whisper, everything.
full_explanation: Transformers (2019+) provides a unified Python API for thousands of model architectures. One function — from_pretrained('model-name') — downloads weights from the Hub, loads the matching tokenizer, and returns a runnable model. AutoModel, AutoTokenizer, and pipelines abstract over architectural differences. Along with datasets, accelerate, peft, trl, and diffusers, it forms the backbone of open-source ML tooling.
why_it_matters: Transformers is where most ML workflows start. Not knowing it is like not knowing requests in Python.

title: Hugging Face Hub
short_definition: The GitHub-of-ML-models — hosts 1M+ model, dataset, and space repositories with git-LFS versioning, model cards, and a unified loading API.
full_explanation: The Hub (huggingface.co) centralizes AI artifacts. Models, datasets, and Spaces (demo apps) all live in git repos backed by git-LFS for large files. Model cards document training data, intended use, and limitations — often mandated for publication. The Hub powers virtually all open-source model distribution and is the backbone of the HF ecosystem. Custom private hubs (Hugging Face Enterprise) host weights inside corporate perimeters.
why_it_matters: The Hub is the center of gravity for open-source ML. Every serious ML workflow interacts with it directly or indirectly.

title: Hugging Face Datasets
short_definition: A library for loading, processing, and streaming ML datasets with memory-mapped access and deep Hub integration.
full_explanation: datasets (Hugging Face) provides a unified interface for 300k+ datasets on the Hub. Arrow-backed memory mapping lets you load datasets larger than RAM. Streaming mode reads from cloud storage without full download — essential for pretraining corpora. map/filter/select ops are parallelizable; dataset scripts are reproducible with a version pin. Integrates with Trainer and Accelerate.
why_it_matters: If your data is on HF, this library is the right way to load it. Skip the CSV download and pandas round-trip.

title: Accelerate (Hugging Face)
short_definition: A lightweight HF library that abstracts distributed training backends (DDP, FSDP, DeepSpeed, Megatron) behind a small common API.
full_explanation: Accelerate (2022+) lets you write training loops that run on any hardware configuration — single GPU, multi-GPU, multi-node, TPU — without framework-specific code. notebook_launcher, accelerate launch, and accelerator.prepare() are the typical entry points. Under the hood it delegates to PyTorch DDP/FSDP, DeepSpeed, or MegatronLM. Supports mixed precision, gradient accumulation, and gradient clipping uniformly.
why_it_matters: Accelerate removes most of the boilerplate from distributed training. A common default for HF-ecosystem work.

title: Unsloth
short_definition: An open-source LLM fine-tuning library that delivers 2x training speed and 80% memory savings on consumer GPUs through custom Triton kernels and memory optimizations.
full_explanation: Unsloth rewrites critical forward/backward kernels (attention, MLP, cross-entropy) in Triton and aggressively reuses activations. Results: fine-tuning Llama-3-8B on a single 24GB consumer GPU without offloading, or Llama-3.1-70B on a 48GB card with QLoRA. Works with LoRA, QLoRA, and DPO via the TRL integration. The go-to choice for indie developers and researchers fine-tuning on consumer hardware.
why_it_matters: Unsloth is what makes consumer-GPU fine-tuning practical at 2026 model sizes.

title: Axolotl
short_definition: A YAML-configured LLM fine-tuning framework covering SFT, LoRA, QLoRA, DPO, ORPO, full fine-tune, and multi-GPU training — the Swiss Army knife of LLM training.
full_explanation: Axolotl wraps Transformers, TRL, DeepSpeed, and FSDP under a simple YAML config: define base model, dataset format, training method, and hardware setup. Supports ShareGPT and Alpaca formats, ChatML templates, and most modern PEFT methods. Strong community with templates for popular training recipes. Common choice for teams fine-tuning multiple models without writing custom code per run.
why_it_matters: Axolotl covers 90% of real-world fine-tuning needs with a config file. Faster path to shipping a fine-tune than hand-rolled training loops.

title: TRL (Transformers Reinforcement Learning)
short_definition: Hugging Face's library for RLHF and preference optimization — SFTTrainer, DPOTrainer, PPOTrainer, KTOTrainer, RewardTrainer.
full_explanation: TRL (2020+, rewritten 2023) implements the post-training pipeline: SFT, reward modeling, PPO, DPO, KTO, ORPO, CPO, and iterative methods. Each trainer takes a model, tokenizer, and dataset. The library composes cleanly with Accelerate and PEFT for distributed PEFT training. Used by most open-source post-training recipes and tutorials.
why_it_matters: TRL is the default post-training library in the HF ecosystem. If you are aligning a model, this is likely your starting point.

title: PEFT Library (HF)
short_definition: Hugging Face's library implementing LoRA, QLoRA, DoRA, adapters, prompt tuning, IA3, and most major PEFT methods behind a unified API.
full_explanation: PEFT (peft library) wraps any Transformers model with a PEFT config — e.g., LoraConfig with target_modules and rank — returning a wrapped model that only trains the specified parameters. Supports merging adapters back into the base for inference, loading/saving adapters separately, and multi-adapter composition. Integrates with Trainer, Accelerate, and TRL.
why_it_matters: PEFT library is the de facto implementation of LoRA and friends. Most open-source fine-tuning tutorials use it directly.

title: BitsAndBytes
short_definition: A library for 8-bit and 4-bit quantization of LLMs at load time, enabling bigger models to fit on smaller GPUs via drop-in Transformers integration.
full_explanation: bitsandbytes (Dettmers 2022+) provides LLM.int8() 8-bit quantization with mixed-precision compute, NF4 and FP4 formats for 4-bit, and double-quantization for extra savings. Integrated with Transformers via `load_in_8bit=True` or a BitsAndBytesConfig. Core enabler of QLoRA. Quality impact is negligible for most use cases; inference speed improves on memory-bound workloads.
why_it_matters: BitsAndBytes is the simplest path to running a big model on a small GPU. Used in countless fine-tuning tutorials.

title: Diffusers (Library)
short_definition: Hugging Face's library for diffusion models — Stable Diffusion, FLUX, Sora-style, SVD — with a consistent API across image, video, and audio generation.
full_explanation: diffusers (2022+) provides the Transformers-equivalent ergonomics for diffusion: pipelines (StableDiffusionPipeline, FluxPipeline), schedulers (DPM++, Euler, DDIM), and fine-tuning utilities (DreamBooth, LoRA). Supports nearly every notable open-source image/video generation model. Integrates with Accelerate for multi-GPU inference and training.
why_it_matters: For diffusion model work in Python, diffusers is the default library. Most pipelines ship reference implementations there.

title: LangChain
short_definition: A Python/JS framework for building LLM applications — chains, agents, retrievers, memory, and 700+ integrations with models, DBs, and tools.
full_explanation: LangChain (2022+) provides high-level abstractions for prompt templates, chains (pipelines of LLM calls), agents (ReAct loops with tools), retrievers, memory, and output parsers. Very large ecosystem — 700+ integrations. Often criticized for abstraction bloat; LangGraph (same authors) is a more explicit graph-based alternative for complex flows. Still the most widely adopted LLM-app framework in Python.
why_it_matters: LangChain is where most LLM app tutorials start. Knowing its core abstractions is table stakes even if you later move off it.

title: LangGraph
short_definition: LangChain's graph-based orchestration library for building stateful, multi-agent LLM workflows with explicit node/edge semantics and durable execution.
full_explanation: LangGraph (2024+) replaces LangChain's chain abstraction with a proper graph: nodes are functions or agents, edges encode transitions, state is a typed shared object. Supports conditional branching, loops with step budgets, checkpoints (durable execution), and human-in-the-loop interruption. Common choice for production agents where LangChain's chain model is too implicit.
why_it_matters: For production agent workflows, LangGraph is LangChain's answer to growing complexity. A better default than raw chains for multi-step agents.

title: LlamaIndex
short_definition: A framework optimized for RAG — indexing, retrieval, query engines, and agent workflows with a focus on connecting LLMs to private data.
full_explanation: LlamaIndex (2022+) started as GPT-Index, a minimal retrieval library, and has grown into a full LLM-app framework. Core abstractions: documents, nodes (chunks), indexes (vector, keyword, tree, knowledge graph), retrievers, query engines, and agents. More RAG-focused than LangChain. The LlamaHub connector marketplace and LlamaParse (document parser) are notable ecosystem pieces.
why_it_matters: If your app is primarily RAG, LlamaIndex is often a cleaner starting point than LangChain.

title: DSPy
short_definition: Stanford's framework for programming (not prompting) LLMs — you write modules and signatures, DSPy compiles and optimizes the prompts automatically.
full_explanation: DSPy (Khattab et al. 2023) treats prompts as free parameters and LLM modules as differentiable-ish components. You define module signatures (typed input/output) and compose them; a 'teleprompter' optimizer tunes prompts and few-shot examples against a metric on training data. The result is an LLM pipeline that improves automatically when you swap models or add data. Particularly strong for complex pipelines like multi-hop QA.
why_it_matters: DSPy points at a future where you stop hand-crafting prompts. Worth learning as the state of the art evolves.

title: AutoGen
short_definition: Microsoft's multi-agent conversation framework where agents with distinct roles collaborate to solve tasks via structured dialogue.
full_explanation: AutoGen (Microsoft Research, 2023+) lets you define agents (LLM-backed with a persona and tool set) and orchestrate them via conversation. Typical patterns: Assistant-UserProxy (one agent plans, another executes), group chats with a moderator, hierarchical teams. Notable for the 'two-agent' pattern where one agent codes and another runs code to verify. AutoGen Studio provides a GUI for non-programmers.
why_it_matters: AutoGen is a common default for multi-agent orchestration. Its patterns appear across many production agent systems.

title: CrewAI
short_definition: A Python framework for multi-agent orchestration where agents have roles, goals, and backstories, collaborating through defined processes.
full_explanation: CrewAI models teams: define agents with role, goal, backstory, and tools; group them into crews; define tasks and processes (sequential, hierarchical). Emphasizes agent personas and structured collaboration. Popular for vertical agent demos (marketing team, research team). Competes with AutoGen and LangGraph; more opinionated about team structure than either.
why_it_matters: CrewAI is a popular choice for persona-driven multi-agent apps. Useful to know when comparing agent frameworks.

title: Inngest / Temporal / Dagster (Durable Workflows)
short_definition: General-purpose durable execution engines increasingly used to orchestrate long-running, crash-resistant AI agent workflows.
full_explanation: Durable workflow engines (Temporal, Inngest, Dagster, Prefect) persist workflow state so crashes do not lose progress. For LLM agents, this matters: a 30-minute research agent should not restart from scratch if the server restarts. Each has different semantics — Temporal's reflection-based replay, Inngest's function-based steps, Dagster's asset-first design — but all provide step memoization, retries, and observability.
why_it_matters: Durable workflows are how agent systems become reliable at scale. A growing best practice for production agents.

title: Modal (Serverless GPU)
short_definition: A serverless GPU platform where you write Python functions with decorators and Modal handles containers, GPUs, scaling, and cold starts.
full_explanation: Modal (2022+) gives you the ergonomics of AWS Lambda but with GPU access. Decorate a Python function with @stub.function(gpu='A100'); Modal builds a container, schedules it on a GPU when called, and scales to zero when idle. Pricing per GPU-second. Used heavily by indie developers and startups for Whisper deployments, image generation APIs, and LLM fine-tuning jobs where persistent GPU rental is overkill.
why_it_matters: Modal is the fastest 'Python → GPU endpoint' path. Heavily used in the 2024–2026 indie ML scene.

title: RunPod
short_definition: A GPU cloud platform offering on-demand and spot instances at discount prices, with serverless endpoints for inference workloads.
full_explanation: RunPod focuses on price-per-GPU-hour with a marketplace of community and secure cloud GPUs. Offers pod rental for persistent workloads (training, dev) and 'RunPod Serverless' for scale-to-zero inference endpoints where you pay per request. Popular with indie developers and smaller teams as an alternative to AWS/GCP GPU instances.
why_it_matters: RunPod is a standard choice for budget-conscious GPU access. Worth knowing alongside Modal and Lambda.

title: Ray (Distributed Python)
short_definition: Anyscale's distributed Python framework — actors, tasks, and Ray Serve/Train/Tune — used for scaling ML workloads across clusters.
full_explanation: Ray provides a unified API for distributed Python. Core: remote tasks and stateful actors. Libraries: Ray Data (distributed data loading), Ray Train (distributed training), Ray Tune (hyperparameter search), Ray Serve (online serving), RLlib (RL). KubeRay integrates with Kubernetes. Common for large-scale ML pipelines where raw PyTorch distributed is insufficient.
why_it_matters: Ray is the default distributed-compute layer for many mid-to-large ML teams. Especially strong for mixed workloads.

title: Ray Serve
short_definition: Ray's model-serving library with native Python APIs, dynamic batching, autoscaling, and deployment graph composition.
full_explanation: Ray Serve lets you deploy any Python function/class as a scalable endpoint. Define @serve.deployment with replicas and resource requirements; compose multiple deployments into graphs with routing logic. Supports batching, multi-model inference, and streaming. Integrates with FastAPI for HTTP. Commonly used for serving complex ML pipelines that are not just single model calls.
why_it_matters: Ray Serve is a flexible, Python-native serving option for composite ML systems.

title: BentoML
short_definition: An open-source framework for packaging and serving ML models as REST/gRPC endpoints with Docker-ready deployment artifacts.
full_explanation: BentoML lets you define a 'service' from a model, wrap it with input/output schemas, and build a 'bento' — a versioned artifact containing model, code, and environment specs. BentoCloud or Yatai handle deployment to Kubernetes. Supports dynamic batching, adaptive scaling, and micro-batching. A pragmatic middle ground between custom Flask/FastAPI services and heavyweight Triton deployments.
why_it_matters: BentoML is a popular choice for Python-first ML serving with good operational ergonomics.

title: Kubernetes (ML Workloads)
short_definition: The default container orchestration platform for production ML — GPU scheduling, autoscaling, job lifecycle, and multi-tenant isolation.
full_explanation: Kubernetes is where most production ML lives. GPU pods, node taints for accelerator types, Volcano or Kueue for batch training, KServe/KubeRay/Seldon for inference. Operators abstract ML-specific concerns: Kubeflow for pipelines, Argo for workflows, NVIDIA GPU Operator for driver management. Most cloud-neutral ML deployments target Kubernetes.
why_it_matters: Any non-trivial production ML deployment eventually touches Kubernetes. Knowing the basics is table stakes.

title: Docker (ML Containers)
short_definition: The standard container format for packaging ML code, models, and dependencies into reproducible, deployable images.
full_explanation: Docker images package everything an ML model needs: Python, CUDA, framework versions, model weights, inference code. Base images: nvidia/cuda, pytorch/pytorch, tensorflow/tensorflow, or the leaner NVIDIA NGC catalog. Key patterns: multi-stage builds to keep images small, BuildKit for layer caching, NVIDIA Container Toolkit for GPU passthrough. Underlies Kubernetes, Docker Compose, and most cloud ML services.
why_it_matters: Every production ML system ships as Docker images. Knowing the basics is essential.

title: Context Length Extension
short_definition: Post-training techniques that extend a model's usable context window beyond its training length, typically via RoPE scaling plus a short fine-tune.
full_explanation: A model trained at 4k tokens loses quality past that length. Extension methods: (1) rescale RoPE frequencies (NTK-aware, YaRN), (2) fine-tune on longer sequences with the rescaled position encoding, (3) use sliding-window or streaming attention for the long-context layers. Typical recipe: YaRN scaling + 1B tokens of fine-tuning on long documents extends 4k → 64k or 128k with minimal quality loss.
why_it_matters: Context extension is how most open-source long-context models got their long context. Understanding it clarifies quality trade-offs.

title: Lost in the Middle
short_definition: A long-context failure pattern where models use information at the beginning and end of context well but ignore content in the middle.
full_explanation: Liu et al. (2023) documented 'lost in the middle' across many LLMs: if the key fact is at the start or end of context, models retrieve it reliably; if it is in the middle of a long context, retrieval accuracy drops 30–40 percentage points. Partly an attention-pattern artifact, partly a training-data artifact. Mitigation: place critical info first or last, use retrieval to surface relevant content to the beginning, or use models specifically trained on balanced long-context.
why_it_matters: Long-context models are often worse in the middle. Design prompts accordingly — do not rely on 'the model will read everything.'

title: LongBench / RULER
short_definition: Benchmarks that stress-test long-context LLMs with multi-hop QA, retrieval, summarization, and synthetic tasks up to hundreds of thousands of tokens.
full_explanation: LongBench (Tsinghua 2023) evaluates long-context tasks across single-doc QA, multi-doc QA, summarization, few-shot learning, and synthetic key-value retrieval. RULER (Nvidia 2024) extends to 128k+ tokens with multiple needle-in-haystack variants, multi-hop tracing, and word extraction under distractors. Together they give a more rigorous picture of long-context ability than NIAH alone.
why_it_matters: If a model claims 128k context, RULER and LongBench tell you whether it actually uses that context. Read these before trusting context-length marketing.

title: GSM8K
short_definition: OpenAI's 8k-example grade-school math word-problem benchmark — the starter reasoning eval that most LLMs now saturate.
full_explanation: GSM8K (Cobbe et al. 2021) tests multi-step arithmetic word problems ranging from 2–8 reasoning steps. Classic LLMs were near zero without chain-of-thought; CoT unlocked the benchmark and pushed scores past 90% for frontier models. Now largely considered saturated but still a useful sanity check. Harder successors: MATH (competition problems), GPQA (graduate-level science), AIME-level math.
why_it_matters: GSM8K is the reasoning benchmark everyone knows. A useful reference point when reading model cards.

title: MATH (Competition Math Benchmark)
short_definition: A 12,500-problem benchmark of competition math (AMC/AIME-level) — much harder than GSM8K and still unsaturated for most models.
full_explanation: MATH (Hendrycks et al. 2021) covers algebra, calculus, number theory, and geometry at competition difficulty. Answers are exact; evaluation uses normalized string match or a checker LLM. Historically stood as an unsaturated benchmark for years; reasoning models (o1, DeepSeek-R1) pushed scores past 90% in 2024–2025 via RL on math. Still useful for under-6B-parameter models where it remains hard.
why_it_matters: MATH is the reasoning-difficulty benchmark just above GSM8K. Frontier reasoning models are measured on it.

title: GPQA (Graduate-Level Google-Proof QA)
short_definition: A 448-question benchmark of graduate-level science (physics, biology, chemistry) that PhDs without internet access score ~65% on — a hard reasoning test.
full_explanation: GPQA (Rein et al. 2023) asks questions written and verified by PhD-level experts. Designed to be 'Google-proof' — answers cannot be looked up directly. Non-expert humans with internet access score ~30%; PhDs score ~65%; frontier models (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, o1) reach 50–65%. A premier benchmark for measuring reasoning beyond MATH.
why_it_matters: GPQA is where frontier-model reasoning differences show up. Seeing it reported is now standard in frontier launches.

title: MMLU-Pro
short_definition: A harder, less-contaminated successor to MMLU with 10-way multiple choice and harder questions, reducing test-taking-skill effects.
full_explanation: MMLU-Pro (Wang et al. 2024) expands the answer choices from 4 to 10 (reducing random-guess success), filters out easy questions, and adds deep-reasoning questions from textbook problems. Replaces MMLU as the primary broad-knowledge LLM benchmark. Scores are roughly 20–30 points lower than MMLU for the same model, giving more room to differentiate models.
why_it_matters: MMLU-Pro has replaced MMLU as the knowledge-benchmark reference in 2024–2026 model launches.

title: AgentBench / AgentEval
short_definition: Benchmarks that evaluate LLM agents on multi-step tasks in realistic environments — shells, browsers, databases, OSes — rather than single-shot Q&A.
full_explanation: AgentBench (Liu et al. 2023), WebArena, OSWorld, SWE-Bench, and τ-Bench evaluate agents on end-to-end tasks: book a flight via a real website, fix a real repo bug, complete an OS-level administrative task. Pass rates are far lower than on text-only benchmarks — 20–50% even for frontier models on hard tasks. Agent benchmarks are where 2025–2026 research progress is most visible.
why_it_matters: If your product is agentic, traditional benchmarks tell you little. Agent benchmarks are the right reference.

title: LiveCodeBench
short_definition: A continually updated coding benchmark that sources problems from LeetCode, AtCoder, and Codeforces after each model's training cutoff to avoid contamination.
full_explanation: LiveCodeBench (Jain et al. 2024) addresses the saturation-and-contamination problem of HumanEval. It continuously adds new competitive programming problems posted after known model cutoffs, rolling forward to keep benchmarks honest. Evaluates generation, self-repair, execution, and test-output prediction. The go-to unambiguous coding benchmark in 2024–2026.
why_it_matters: LiveCodeBench is the uncontaminated successor to HumanEval. Read it for current coding model comparisons.

title: BigCodeBench
short_definition: A coding benchmark that evaluates LLMs on tasks requiring library usage and complex instruction following — a more realistic test than HumanEval.
full_explanation: BigCodeBench (Zhuo et al. 2024) includes 1,140 tasks that require using 139 Python libraries, with rigorous unit tests and branch-coverage checks. Tasks include data processing, web scraping, machine learning, and system admin — not just algorithmic puzzles. Complete/Instruct splits test completion from function signature vs. full-natural-language descriptions. Scores are typically 30–50 points lower than HumanEval, leaving room to differentiate.
why_it_matters: BigCodeBench captures realistic coding ability better than HumanEval. A good companion to LiveCodeBench.

title: Tau-Bench (τ-Bench)
short_definition: A benchmark from Sierra that evaluates LLM agents on realistic customer-support tasks over APIs with a simulated user and success criteria.
full_explanation: τ-Bench (Yao et al. 2024) simulates domains — airline customer service, retail order support — where agents must interact with a user (another LLM role-playing) and real APIs (tool calls to a mock backend). Success is measured by state match after the conversation (e.g., reservation correctly modified) plus instruction compliance. Current frontier-model pass rates hover around 50–70% for single-task attempts, dropping on consecutive runs. A production-flavored agent eval.
why_it_matters: τ-Bench is one of the best public benchmarks for customer-support-style agents. Worth studying when building real agent products.

title: SWE-Agent / Agentless
short_definition: Agent frameworks designed to maximize SWE-Bench performance — SWE-Agent via custom agent-computer interface, Agentless via simpler structured pipelines.
full_explanation: SWE-Agent (Princeton, 2024) provides a custom 'Agent-Computer Interface' of commands tuned for code editing, making the LLM more reliable on repo tasks. Agentless (Xia et al. 2024) shows a simple, non-agentic pipeline — localize files, generate patches, validate — can match SWE-Agent at a fraction of the cost. Both are starting points for building real coding agents and illustrate that 'more autonomy' is not always better.
why_it_matters: Reading these frameworks clarifies how modern coding agents actually work. Useful reference for building your own.

title: Pipeline (LLM Serving Pipeline)
short_definition: The sequence of components (tokenizer, model, sampler, post-processors) packaged together as a single runnable unit behind a common API.
full_explanation: A pipeline abstracts the full inference flow: tokenize input → run model → sample output → decode. Hugging Face's pipeline() is the canonical Python entry point — pipeline('text-generation', model='...') returns a callable that handles all the steps. Pipelines exist for NER, classification, image generation, speech recognition, and many more tasks. Useful for quick prototyping but often too limiting for production.
why_it_matters: pipeline() is the fastest path from 'I want to try this model' to running inference. Great for exploration.

title: vLLM OpenAI API Server
short_definition: vLLM's built-in OpenAI-compatible REST server that lets any OpenAI SDK client talk to local vLLM-served models.
full_explanation: Starting vLLM with --port 8000 brings up an OpenAI-compatible endpoint at /v1/chat/completions and /v1/completions. Any SDK (openai-python, openai-node) can point to this URL with a fake key and get full LLM functionality. This is the standard pattern for self-hosted open-source LLMs — clients remain OpenAI-compatible while backends are swapped freely.
