why_it_matters: OpenAI-compatible servers are how you migrate code from OpenAI to self-hosted without rewriting clients. vLLM is the most common option.

title: Quantization-Aware Training (QAT)
short_definition: Training a model with simulated quantization in the forward pass so the weights learn to be robust to the precision loss of actual quantization.
full_explanation: Instead of quantizing a trained full-precision model (PTQ), QAT quantizes and dequantizes weights and activations during training's forward pass. Gradients still flow as if full precision. The model adjusts to the quantization noise during training, yielding substantially better quality at low bits (2–3 bit) than PTQ alone. BitNet b1.58 (Microsoft, 2024) used QAT for ternary weights. Expensive to implement but enables extreme quantization.
why_it_matters: QAT is the path to very low-bit models (1–3 bit) with usable quality. Worth knowing as sub-4-bit inference becomes standard.

title: BitNet (1.58-bit Models)
short_definition: Microsoft's research line on LLMs with ternary weights ({-1, 0, +1}) trained with QAT, achieving near-parity with bf16 at a fraction of memory and inference cost.
full_explanation: BitNet b1.58 (Ma et al. 2024) quantizes weights to ternary values during training and uses specialized inference kernels that replace matmul with addition. Memory drops 10x vs. bf16; inference throughput can rise 3–4x on supported kernels. Quality is surprisingly close to full precision at 3B–70B scale. An active research frontier with promising economic implications for inference.
why_it_matters: BitNet shows extreme quantization can be competitive. If it scales further, it changes the economics of inference.

title: Grokking
short_definition: A training phenomenon where a model overfits for a long time and then suddenly generalizes — a dramatic phase transition in generalization.
full_explanation: Grokking (Power et al. 2022) was discovered training small transformers on modular arithmetic. Training accuracy hits 100% in a few thousand steps; validation accuracy stays at chance for millions of steps, then jumps to 100% — grokking. Mech-interp analyses (Anthropic, Neel Nanda) found the model is learning generalizing circuits under the hood during the long plateau. Connected to neural network double descent, and a useful window into how generalization emerges.
why_it_matters: Grokking is a puzzle with deep implications for what generalization is. A touchstone for interpretability research.

title: Double Descent
short_definition: A phenomenon where test error decreases, then increases (classical overfitting), then decreases again as model capacity or training time grows past a threshold.
full_explanation: Classical statistical learning predicts a U-shaped test error curve as capacity grows: underfit, sweet spot, overfit. Double descent (Belkin et al. 2019) shows that past the interpolation threshold (capacity ≥ training-set size), test error falls again — often below the classical sweet spot. Explains why massively overparameterized deep networks generalize well. Applies to model size (capacity), training steps, and sample size in different forms.
why_it_matters: Double descent is why 'just make it bigger' works in deep learning. Foundational for understanding LLM scaling.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting
short_definition: The tendency of neural networks to lose previously learned capabilities when trained on new data, especially during fine-tuning.
full_explanation: Neural networks do not preserve knowledge by default. Fine-tuning on domain-specific data commonly degrades general capabilities — a model fine-tuned on medical notes might forget how to write code. Mitigations: data mixing (include general data in the fine-tune), lower learning rates, LoRA (only small adapters change), elastic weight consolidation, and replay-based methods. For LLMs, continued pretraining needs careful data mix design to avoid forgetting.
why_it_matters: Every fine-tuning project needs a forgetting-mitigation strategy. Otherwise your specialized model is good at one thing and worse at everything else.

title: Continual Learning
short_definition: The research direction of training models on a stream of new data over time without catastrophic forgetting or needing to retrain from scratch.
full_explanation: Continual learning (a.k.a. lifelong learning) tries to make models update incrementally. Classic methods: elastic weight consolidation (penalize changing important weights), replay buffers (mix new data with old), progressive networks (grow capacity for new tasks). For LLMs, continual learning is largely aspirational — in practice, most teams retrain from checkpoints rather than stream updates. An open problem with significant safety implications (what if a deployed model drifts?).
why_it_matters: Continual learning is the path to persistent-memory agents. Active research area with no consensus solution yet.

title: Agent Memory
short_definition: Mechanisms that let AI agents retain information across turns or sessions — conversation buffers, summary memories, vector stores, scratchpads.
full_explanation: Memory types in agent systems: short-term (recent messages within context), working (scratchpad of current task state), episodic (past task outcomes via vector search), and semantic (distilled facts about the user/world). Frameworks like Mem0, LangMem, Letta (formerly MemGPT), and Zep persist memory outside the LLM. Design trade-offs include retrieval relevance, privacy, and when to evict or summarize.
why_it_matters: Agents without memory restart every conversation. Memory is the difference between a tool and an assistant that learns you.

title: MemGPT (Letta)
short_definition: A memory architecture (now called Letta) that gives LLMs OS-like memory management — paging context in and out of limited working memory.
full_explanation: MemGPT (Packer et al. 2023) treats the LLM's context window like RAM in a virtual-memory OS. Long-term memory lives in external archives; the agent uses explicit tool calls to save, recall, and summarize. This enables conversations with memory far exceeding context limits — effectively unlimited. Renamed to Letta in 2024 as the project grew into a full agent runtime.
why_it_matters: MemGPT is an elegant architecture for memory-heavy agents. Worth studying even if you do not adopt it directly.

title: Mem0
short_definition: An open-source memory layer for AI applications that stores, retrieves, and updates user- and session-level memories across any LLM.
full_explanation: Mem0 provides a simple API: add_memory(user, content), get_memories(user, query). Under the hood it uses vector search plus LLM-driven memory extraction and conflict resolution (new memory contradicts old? update rather than append). Integrates with OpenAI, Anthropic, Claude, and local LLMs. A common choice for adding persistent memory to any chatbot without reinventing the wheel.
why_it_matters: Mem0 is the easiest drop-in memory layer for LLM apps today. Removes most of the plumbing work.

title: Context Engineering
short_definition: The practice of deliberately shaping what ends up in an LLM's context window — retrieval, memory, tool output, and system prompt — to maximize task success.
full_explanation: Context engineering is prompt engineering's mature cousin: rather than crafting one prompt, you design the whole pipeline that fills context at runtime — which documents to retrieve, how to compress conversation history, which tools outputs to summarize. Includes decisions like chunk size, reranking, context ordering (to avoid 'lost in the middle'), and dynamic system-prompt assembly. Increasingly the core skill for shipping LLM apps.
why_it_matters: Good context engineering often matters more than prompt wording or model choice. It is the modern equivalent of feature engineering.

title: Prompt Engineering
short_definition: The practice of designing input text to LLMs to maximize desired output quality — including role setting, instructions, examples, and output format.
full_explanation: Prompt engineering covers system-prompt design, few-shot example selection, role priming, chain-of-thought cueing, output-format specification, delimiters for untrusted content, and many micro-techniques. Best practices evolve as models change — what worked for GPT-3.5 may be unnecessary or counterproductive for Claude 3.5. Mature prompt engineering is empirical: write, test, iterate with small eval sets.
why_it_matters: Prompt engineering is the first thing to try before fine-tuning, RAG, or a bigger model. Often gives 80% of the quality gain for 5% of the work.

title: Meta-Prompting
short_definition: Using an LLM to improve or generate prompts for other LLMs — iteratively refining based on eval feedback or rubric scoring.
full_explanation: Meta-prompting asks the LLM to critique a prompt ('what might be unclear?') or generate variants ('rewrite 5 versions emphasizing concision'). Automated prompt optimization frameworks like DSPy, PromptBreeder, and OPRO use evaluation scores plus LLM-generated variants to search prompt space. Useful for squeezing out last-mile quality when manual iteration plateaus.
why_it_matters: Meta-prompting is the first step toward automated prompt optimization. Becomes routine as eval infrastructure matures.

title: PromptFoo
short_definition: An open-source CLI and framework for testing, evaluating, and red-teaming LLM prompts — think Jest for LLMs.
full_explanation: PromptFoo (2023+) lets you define prompt tests in YAML: prompt templates, model configurations, expected-output assertions (exact match, contains, LLM-as-judge, custom Python). Runs in CI, produces HTML reports comparing models/prompts. Built-in red-teaming modes inject adversarial prompts for safety testing. A production-grade prompt regression tool.
why_it_matters: PromptFoo is the most mature prompt/eval framework available today. Heavy adoption for CI-based prompt regression.

title: DeepEval
short_definition: An open-source pytest-style framework for LLM evaluation — runs metrics (hallucination, relevance, bias, toxicity) as test assertions.
full_explanation: DeepEval (Confident AI) lets you write LLM evals that look like unit tests: assert_test(test_case, [HallucinationMetric(), AnswerRelevancyMetric()]). Built-in evaluators include G-Eval, RAGAS metrics, bias, and toxicity checks. Integrates with pytest, generates coverage reports, and plugs into CI. Popular for teams that want 'tests that pass or fail' rather than dashboards.
why_it_matters: DeepEval is a fit for teams that want LLM quality tests in their existing pytest/CI workflow.

title: OpenTelemetry (GenAI / OpenInference)
short_definition: The emerging standard for tracing LLM applications — OpenTelemetry semantic conventions plus vendor-specific attributes like prompts, tokens, and cost.
full_explanation: OTel-GenAI semantic conventions (2024+) standardize how LLM spans are instrumented: attributes for model name, provider, input/output tokens, cost, prompt content (optional), tool calls. OpenInference (Arize) extends this with more ML-specific schemas. Langfuse, LangSmith, Arize Phoenix, and Helicone all increasingly emit OTel-compatible traces, enabling cross-vendor observability tooling.
why_it_matters: OTel standardization is what will eventually unify LLM observability. Worth building toward even if not fully there yet.

title: Helicone
short_definition: An LLM observability and gateway platform focused on logging, cost tracking, caching, and rate limiting — one proxy line away from adoption.
full_explanation: Helicone proxies your LLM API calls (change the base URL to helicone.ai) and captures full request/response data. Dashboards cover cost by user/project, latency, error rates, and prompt analytics. Features: caching, retries, rate-limit smoothing, and custom property tagging for segmenting by feature. A pragmatic first observability tool for teams that want data without invasive instrumentation.
why_it_matters: Helicone is one of the lowest-friction LLM observability tools. Easy to drop in and get immediate value.

title: Portkey
short_definition: An LLM gateway platform with multi-provider routing, fallbacks, caching, guardrails, and observability — targeted at enterprise LLM infrastructure.
full_explanation: Portkey sits between your app and LLM providers. Configs define routing strategies (load balance across providers), fallbacks (if OpenAI errors, try Anthropic), caching, and guardrail rules. Observability includes cost tracking, request traces, and analytics. Includes a prompt management layer with versioning and A/B testing. Heavier than Helicone but more flexible for complex multi-provider setups.
why_it_matters: Portkey is a leading choice for serious multi-provider LLM deployments where routing and governance matter.

title: Braintrust
short_definition: A platform for LLM evaluation, dataset management, and logging that integrates evaluation into the development workflow like unit tests.
full_explanation: Braintrust provides datasets (versioned, sharable), experiments (run N variants on a dataset with scorers), and logging (production traces linked to offline evals). Scorers are LLM-as-judge or Python functions. Strong prompt-playground UX, good SDK integration with OpenAI and Anthropic. Popular with teams that treat LLM development like ML research — evaluation-driven, not prompt-driven.
why_it_matters: Braintrust is a strong evaluation-first tool. Fits well when you want discipline around LLM quality measurement.

title: Inference as a Service
short_definition: Managed inference endpoints from providers (Together, Fireworks, Groq, Replicate, Anyscale) that host open-source LLMs behind an OpenAI-compatible API.
full_explanation: Inference-as-a-service providers host popular open-source LLMs on shared or dedicated GPUs and bill per token. Examples: Together AI, Fireworks AI, Groq (custom LPUs for extreme-speed inference), Replicate, Anyscale, Deepinfra, NVIDIA NIM. Benefits: no infra to manage, pay-per-use, instant access to many models. Drawbacks: privacy, cost at scale, dependency on provider.
why_it_matters: Inference-as-a-service is often the first step when moving beyond OpenAI/Anthropic to open-source models.

title: Groq (LPU)
short_definition: Groq's custom Language Processing Units and their inference service, achieving extremely high tokens-per-second throughput (500+ tok/s) on popular LLMs.
full_explanation: Groq LPUs are custom chips with deterministic memory and compute designed specifically for inference. They produce very high token throughput — often 5–10x faster than GPU-based serving for the same model — with predictable latency. The Groq Cloud hosts Llama, Mixtral, and other open models at these speeds. Trade-offs: smaller model selection, no support for latest huge models yet.
why_it_matters: For latency-critical LLM applications, Groq's speeds can be transformative. Worth evaluating for real-time voice or interactive UX.

title: Together AI
short_definition: A major inference-as-a-service provider hosting a wide catalog of open-source LLMs with competitive per-token pricing and fine-tuning endpoints.
full_explanation: Together AI hosts hundreds of open-source models — Llama, Mixtral, Qwen, DeepSeek, and more — behind an OpenAI-compatible API. Also offers managed fine-tuning, dedicated endpoints, and GPU clusters. Competes primarily on model breadth and speed. Popular choice for teams that want to run many open models without managing GPUs.
why_it_matters: Together AI is one of the most comprehensive open-model API providers. Often the fastest path to trying a new open model.

title: Fireworks AI
short_definition: An inference provider focused on fast, low-latency open-source LLM serving with fine-tuning, structured output, and vision support.
full_explanation: Fireworks AI (2022+) hosts a curated catalog of open LLMs (Llama, Mixtral, DeepSeek, Qwen VL) with strong emphasis on latency optimizations — their claimed throughput is often top-tier among GPU-based providers. Features: LoRA fine-tuning, grammar-constrained generation, JSON mode, and function calling. Popular with engineering teams that want open-model speed without dedicated GPUs.
why_it_matters: Fireworks is a top choice for latency-sensitive open-model serving. Competitive with or faster than Together on many models.

title: Replicate
short_definition: A model-hosting platform where community-contributed models (LLMs, image gen, video gen, audio) are packaged as 'Cog' containers and served on-demand.
full_explanation: Replicate lets anyone publish a trained model as a Cog container with defined inputs and outputs, then run it via API. Strong in image and video generation (Stable Diffusion, FLUX, SDXL, Hunyuan Video), audio (MusicGen, XTTS), and LLMs. Pricing per second or per run. A go-to option for 'I want to try this weird new model from a paper without installing anything.'
why_it_matters: Replicate is the easiest way to experiment with niche and generative models. Especially strong for media generation.

title: Cog (Replicate)
short_definition: Replicate's open-source tool for packaging ML models into production-ready Docker containers with typed predict functions.
full_explanation: Cog wraps Python ML code into Docker images with a predict() function, typed inputs via Pydantic-style schemas, and automatic API generation. You define cog.yaml (Python version, dependencies, GPU requirements) and predict.py. Builds produce a container runnable on Replicate, Kubernetes, or anywhere Docker runs. A pragmatic alternative to writing Dockerfiles from scratch for ML.
why_it_matters: Cog is the easiest on-ramp to reproducible ML deployment. Even outside Replicate, a useful packaging tool.

title: Gradio
short_definition: A Python library for building ML model demos and web UIs with minimal code — the backbone of most Hugging Face Spaces demos.
full_explanation: Gradio (2019, now part of Hugging Face) lets you build a web UI for a model in a few lines: gr.Interface(fn, inputs, outputs).launch(). Supports text, image, audio, video, and custom components. Shareable public links generate instantly (via a tunnel). Most HF Spaces are Gradio apps. Competes with Streamlit but more ML-demo-focused.
why_it_matters: Gradio is the fastest way to wrap a model in a usable UI for testing, demoing, or sharing. Essential part of the open ML stack.

title: Streamlit
short_definition: A Python library for building data apps and dashboards with pure Python — a common choice for internal LLM tools and prototypes.
full_explanation: Streamlit (2019, Snowflake since 2022) turns Python scripts into interactive web apps. Widgets (st.text_input, st.slider) and layout primitives compose into full apps; state management is reactive. Commonly used for internal dashboards, prompt playgrounds, RAG demos, and model comparison tools. Competes with Gradio; Streamlit is broader (data apps generally) while Gradio is more ML-demo-focused.
why_it_matters: Streamlit is the fastest route from Python notebook to shareable internal tool. Ubiquitous for data-team prototyping.

title: FastAPI (LLM Backends)
short_definition: The dominant Python framework for building production LLM APIs — async-native, Pydantic-typed, OpenAPI-generated.
full_explanation: FastAPI (2018+) became the default for building LLM backends because it plays well with async IO (essential for streaming responses and concurrent LLM calls), validates inputs/outputs via Pydantic, and auto-generates OpenAPI docs. Common pattern: FastAPI handles HTTP, background tasks, and streaming; the underlying LLM calls go to vLLM, OpenAI, or Anthropic. Paired with Uvicorn or Hypercorn for ASGI serving.
why_it_matters: FastAPI is the default Python backend framework for LLM apps. Knowing it is near-mandatory for production work.

title: Pydantic
short_definition: Python's most popular data-validation library — models with typed fields that parse, validate, and serialize data, heavily used for LLM inputs/outputs.
full_explanation: Pydantic (v1, v2) defines data models via Python type hints. Models validate input data, convert types, and serialize cleanly to JSON. For LLMs, Pydantic models are the go-to way to specify structured output schemas (Pydantic model → JSON Schema → constrained decoding). Frameworks like LangChain, LlamaIndex, FastAPI, and TRL all use it. Pydantic AI (2024) targets LLM apps specifically.
why_it_matters: Pydantic is the schema glue between Python and LLMs. Foundational for any production LLM pipeline.

title: Pydantic AI
short_definition: The Pydantic team's framework for building LLM agents with the same ergonomics as Pydantic models — typed inputs, validated outputs, dependency injection.
full_explanation: Pydantic AI (2024) treats agent design as schema-first: declare Agent classes with result_type Pydantic models, tool functions with typed parameters, and dependency injection for shared state. Supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Ollama. Emphasis on production ergonomics, type safety, and testing. A clean alternative to LangChain for teams already deep in the Pydantic/FastAPI ecosystem.
why_it_matters: Pydantic AI is gaining traction as the 'type-first' agent framework. Worth knowing alongside LangGraph.

title: Instructor (Pydantic for LLMs)
short_definition: A Python library that makes LLMs return Pydantic-validated structured outputs with automatic retries and schema-based prompting.
full_explanation: Instructor (Jason Liu, 2023+) wraps OpenAI, Anthropic, and other SDKs so you can pass response_model=SomeModel and get back a validated instance. Under the hood it uses function-calling or JSON mode plus automatic retry on validation errors. Supports streaming (yield partial validated objects as they arrive), partial models, and unions. Widely used for extraction and structured generation.
why_it_matters: Instructor is the most popular structured-output library in Python. Minimal setup, massive win on reliability.

title: BAML (Boundary AI Markup Language)
short_definition: A domain-specific language for defining LLM functions with typed inputs/outputs, prompt templates, and test cases — compiled to Python or TypeScript.
full_explanation: BAML (Boundary, 2023+) lets you define LLM 'functions' in .baml files: declare input/output schemas, prompt templates, test cases. The compiler generates Python or TypeScript bindings. Benefits: type safety across languages, first-class prompt versioning, built-in testing, and deterministic retries. A structured alternative to string-prompt code scattered across a codebase.
why_it_matters: BAML is one of the more interesting attempts at a proper LLM-DSL. Worth evaluating for teams building many typed LLM functions.

title: Voice Agent
short_definition: An AI system that takes voice input and produces voice output in real time, often end-to-end through a multimodal LLM or via ASR → LLM → TTS pipeline.
full_explanation: Voice agents are either cascaded — ASR (Whisper or Deepgram) → LLM → TTS (ElevenLabs, Play.ht, Cartesia) — or native-multimodal (GPT-4o Realtime, Gemini Live). Native models avoid transcription errors and preserve prosody but are newer and more expensive. Cascaded systems are older, cheaper, and more debuggable. Latency targets sub-500ms end-to-end for natural conversation. Frameworks: LiveKit, Vapi, Pipecat, Retell AI, Vocode.
why_it_matters: Voice agents are one of the fastest-growing production LLM use cases in 2024–2026 (call centers, in-car, kiosks). Know the architecture.

title: Text-to-Speech (TTS)
short_definition: Synthesizing natural-sounding speech from text — modern models include ElevenLabs, OpenAI TTS, Cartesia Sonic, XTTS, and open-source F5-TTS.
full_explanation: TTS has evolved from concatenative and formant synthesis to neural waveform generation (WaveNet), then text-to-mel + vocoder (Tacotron + HiFi-GAN), and now end-to-end diffusion/flow models (VALL-E, XTTS, F5-TTS). Modern neural TTS is near-indistinguishable from human in controlled conditions. Voice cloning, emotion control, and streaming generation are the current frontiers. Commercial leaders: ElevenLabs, Cartesia; open source: XTTS-v2, F5-TTS, Fish Speech.
why_it_matters: TTS is the voice of every voice agent. Quality and latency choices shape user experience directly.

title: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR)
short_definition: Transcribing speech to text — dominated by OpenAI Whisper and specialized commercial providers like Deepgram and AssemblyAI.
full_explanation: Modern ASR uses encoder-decoder transformers trained on large weakly supervised audio-text corpora. Word error rate (WER) on clean read speech is below 3% for strong models in English. Challenges: noisy audio, accents, code-switching, technical jargon, speaker diarization, and punctuation. Real-time ASR requires streaming architectures with low lookahead. Commercial: Deepgram Nova, AssemblyAI Universal, Rev, Google STT. Open: Whisper family, NeMo Canary, wav2vec 2.0.
why_it_matters: ASR is half of every voice agent. Quality here directly caps downstream LLM and TTS quality.

title: Diarization (Speaker Diarization)
short_definition: Determining 'who spoke when' in a recording — clustering speech segments by speaker identity without knowing speakers in advance.
full_explanation: Diarization takes audio and outputs timestamped segments labeled by anonymous speaker IDs (Speaker 1, Speaker 2, ...). Modern pipelines: voice activity detection → speaker embeddings (ECAPA-TDNN, pyannote) → clustering → refinement. Paired with ASR it produces a readable multi-speaker transcript. Pyannote.audio is the dominant open-source toolkit; commercial providers bundle it with their ASR.
why_it_matters: Diarization is essential for meeting transcripts, interviews, and any multi-speaker audio. Non-trivial to do well.

title: Voice Activity Detection (VAD)
short_definition: Detecting when speech is present in an audio stream — the trigger that tells voice agents when to start/stop listening.
full_explanation: VAD models (Silero VAD, WebRTC VAD, Deepgram VAD) output per-frame probabilities of speech vs. non-speech. In voice agents, VAD decides when to send audio to ASR and when to trigger the LLM response after the user stops speaking. Tuning end-of-turn detection is subtle — too fast cuts users off, too slow feels laggy. Semantic VAD uses LLMs to decide on turn completion based on content, not just silence.
why_it_matters: VAD tuning is the difference between a voice agent that feels natural and one that feels broken. Critical for conversational UX.

title: Knowledge Graph (RAG Context)
short_definition: A structured representation of entities and relationships — used as a complement or alternative to vector search for multi-hop reasoning.
full_explanation: Knowledge graphs (Neo4j, TigerGraph, Amazon Neptune, RDF stores) represent data as nodes (entities) and edges (relationships). For RAG, you can extract entities and relationships from text corpora and query them via Cypher/SPARQL. GraphRAG patterns combine graph traversal with embedding search, using the graph for multi-hop connections and vectors for fuzzy matching. More structured than pure vector RAG but more engineering-heavy.
why_it_matters: For multi-hop QA and connected-data analysis, knowledge graphs often beat pure vector RAG. Worth the upfront cost for dense domains.

title: Text-to-SQL
short_definition: Translating natural-language questions into SQL queries against a known database schema — one of the oldest practical LLM use cases.
full_explanation: Text-to-SQL prompts the LLM with the schema (tables, columns, relationships) and the user question, expecting valid SQL back. Quality depends heavily on schema presentation, sample rows, and tricky constructs (joins, CTEs, window functions). Frameworks: Vanna.AI, LangChain SQL Agent, LlamaIndex Text-to-SQL. Key evals: BIRD, Spider, BIRD-SQL. Production systems add schema-aware retrieval (only show tables relevant to the question), query validation, and LLM-based retry on errors.
why_it_matters: Text-to-SQL is a high-value enterprise LLM use case. Many 'chat with your data' products are this under the hood.

title: Code Interpreter
short_definition: An LLM tool that writes and executes Python (or other) code in a sandbox, then uses the results — for calculation, data analysis, and reliable reasoning.
full_explanation: Code interpreters (OpenAI Code Interpreter / Advanced Data Analysis, Anthropic code execution, Gemini Code Execution) let the LLM sidestep its arithmetic and logic weaknesses by running actual Python. The model writes code, a sandboxed Python kernel runs it, the output is fed back. Works brilliantly for calculations, plotting, and tabular analysis. Open-source equivalents: E2B, Riza, Jupyter AI. Security requires strict sandboxing — code interpreters are a common attack surface.
why_it_matters: For numerical, tabular, or deterministic-logic tasks, code interpreters dramatically outperform plain LLMs. A standard capability now.

title: Sandbox (LLM Code Execution)
short_definition: Isolated environments where LLM-generated code runs safely — typically Docker containers, Firecracker microVMs, or WebAssembly.
full_explanation: Sandboxes isolate LLM-generated code from the host system: no internet by default, restricted filesystem, no ability to escape. Providers: E2B (Firecracker-based microVMs), Riza (WASM), Replit's sandbox, Modal's sandboxes, and custom Docker setups. Must handle resource limits (CPU, RAM, runtime), network policy, file persistence, and concurrent session isolation. A core primitive for any agent that runs code.
why_it_matters: Any agent that executes LLM code needs a sandbox. Getting this right is both a security and product concern.

title: Browser Use / Computer Use
short_definition: An LLM capability where the model controls a computer GUI — clicks, types, scrolls — via screenshot input and mouse/keyboard output, enabling browser and OS automation.
full_explanation: Anthropic Computer Use (Oct 2024) introduced pixel-level GUI control by Claude: screenshot in, actions out. OpenAI Operator and Google Project Mariner followed. Open-source alternatives: browser-use, Nanobrowser, WebSuite. The model reasons visually about the screen and executes actions step-by-step, often with a planner-executor split. Slower and less reliable than API-based automation but universal — any application becomes usable.
why_it_matters: Computer use is how agents bridge to non-API-accessible applications. A fundamental new LLM capability.

title: Anthropic Workbench / Console
short_definition: Anthropic's web-based prompt development environment for building, testing, and deploying prompts with Claude — equivalent to OpenAI Playground.
full_explanation: Anthropic Console provides a Workbench where you iterate on prompts with Claude, view responses side-by-side across variants, access prompt caching, extended thinking, and tool-use features. Prompts can be saved as 'Prompt Library' entries and called from the API. Also houses the API key management, usage analytics, and experiment tools.
why_it_matters: For Claude-based development, Workbench is where most prompt iteration happens. Useful to know the feature surface.

title: Anthropic API Prompt Caching
short_definition: Claude's explicit prompt-caching mechanism — mark specific content blocks with cache_control and pay a reduced rate on subsequent calls with matching prefixes.
full_explanation: Anthropic's prompt caching requires explicit opt-in: add 'cache_control': {'type': 'ephemeral'} to any content block (system prompt, user message, tool definitions). First call pays a 25% write premium; subsequent calls reusing that prefix pay a 90% discount on the cached tokens. Default TTL is 5 minutes with 1-hour option available. A big lever for agents and RAG with large stable context.
why_it_matters: For stable-prefix workloads on Claude, prompt caching often halves or better costs. Must-use pattern.

title: Extended Thinking (Claude)
short_definition: Anthropic's reasoning mode where Claude produces long internal reasoning before its final answer, toggleable per request with a thinking-token budget.
full_explanation: Extended thinking (Claude 3.7+, 4) lets Claude reason through a problem before responding, similar to OpenAI's o1. Enable via a 'thinking' parameter on the Messages API with a budget_tokens field. The reasoning tokens are visible to the caller (unlike o1's hidden reasoning) and count against output budget. Substantial quality gains on math, code, and multi-step reasoning at proportional cost.
why_it_matters: Extended thinking is Claude's main reasoning-boost feature. Knowing when to enable it and with what budget matters for quality/cost.

title: OpenAI Batch API (Reprise)
short_definition: OpenAI's 50%-discounted asynchronous API for dataset-scale jobs — submit a JSONL file, retrieve results within 24 hours.
full_explanation: Submit a JSONL with one request per line (custom_id + endpoint + body) to the Batch API; each request runs when capacity is available with a 24h completion window. Returns a downloadable JSONL of results. Pricing is 50% off synchronous rates. Ideal for offline evaluations, large synthetic-data generation, and backfilling embeddings.
why_it_matters: For any offline LLM job, Batch API cuts cost in half. A standard optimization for eval and data pipelines.

title: Reasoning Token / Thinking Budget
short_definition: A parameter that caps how many tokens a reasoning model spends on internal reasoning before producing its final answer.
full_explanation: Reasoning models (o1/o3, Claude extended thinking, DeepSeek-R1) can spend large amounts of test-time compute on internal reasoning. A reasoning_effort or thinking_budget parameter (low/medium/high, or explicit token count) lets callers trade quality for cost. High budgets are warranted for complex math/code; low budgets for simple requests. Understanding the trade-off is central to cost-managing reasoning-model deployments.
why_it_matters: Reasoning budgets can 10x costs if left uncapped. A key operations knob in the reasoning-model era.

title: Responses API Reasoning (OpenAI)
short_definition: OpenAI's Responses API surface for reasoning models — provides reasoning_effort control, persistent reasoning summaries, and tool-use integration.
full_explanation: The Responses API is the canonical surface for OpenAI reasoning models (o1, o3, o4-mini and successors). Parameters like reasoning.effort (minimal/low/medium/high) tune test-time compute. Reasoning summaries can be surfaced or kept hidden. Built-in tools (web_search, code_interpreter, file_search) integrate naturally with reasoning loops. Designed for long-running agent tasks that the older Chat Completions API handled awkwardly.
why_it_matters: For OpenAI reasoning models, Responses API is the first-class interface. Chat Completions has limited support.

title: Prompt Injection Defense
short_definition: Techniques for hardening LLM apps against prompt-injection attacks — input sanitization, role-based delimiting, output monitoring, and tool-permission least privilege.
full_explanation: No single defense solves prompt injection. Layered defenses: (1) clearly delimit untrusted input with structural cues the model is trained to respect, (2) keep tool permissions minimal (the model cannot do what it cannot invoke), (3) use separate 'critic' models to check outputs before executing actions, (4) run human-in-the-loop for irreversible operations, (5) log and monitor for anomalous tool-call patterns. Spotlighting (Anthropic) and CaMeL (Google) are recent research on stronger defenses.
why_it_matters: Prompt injection is the top unsolved LLM security problem. Defense in depth is the current state of the art.

title: Jailbreak Defense
short_definition: Techniques for hardening LLMs against user attempts to bypass safety guardrails — from training (RLHF, constitutional AI) to inference (input/output filtering, classifiers).
full_explanation: Jailbreak defenses include: adversarially trained models (using red-team-found jailbreaks as training data), dedicated safety classifiers (Llama Guard, ShieldGemma) that block unsafe inputs or outputs, self-critique loops where the model checks its own response against policy, and circuit-breaker techniques that interrupt unsafe generation. No defense is perfect; the arms race is ongoing.
why_it_matters: Every customer-facing LLM product needs jailbreak defenses. Understanding layered approaches is core safety engineering.

title: Model Spec (OpenAI)
short_definition: OpenAI's public document describing intended model behavior — rules, defaults, and principles for how ChatGPT and API models should respond.
full_explanation: The Model Spec (May 2024) is OpenAI's attempt to publish its alignment targets: a hierarchy of rules (platform > developer > user), default behaviors, and case studies of ambiguous requests. It makes model behavior contestable and debuggable — when the model declines or complies, you can check the spec. Similar public behavior documents exist from Anthropic (Usage Policies, Constitution draft) and Google.
why_it_matters: Reading the Model Spec is the best way to understand why GPT models behave the way they do. Increasingly expected of all frontier labs.

title: Spotlighting (Prompt Injection Defense)
short_definition: A prompt-injection defense that transforms untrusted content into a form (tagging, encoding) making instructions within it harder for the model to follow.
full_explanation: Spotlighting (Hines et al. 2024) makes injection attacks less effective by marking untrusted content with a visible boundary — prefixing each non-space character with a special delimiter, for example. The model can still read the content but is trained (or prompted) to treat such content as data rather than instructions. Works as part of a layered defense but not a complete solution.
why_it_matters: Spotlighting is one of the more practical prompt-injection defenses available. Worth including in a defense stack.

title: CaMeL (Google Prompt Injection Defense)
short_definition: Google's 'Capability-Based Mitigation for LLMs' — a system that separates trusted instructions from untrusted data using a privileged planning model and a restricted execution model.
full_explanation: CaMeL (2024) splits agent work between two LLMs: a privileged planner that operates only on user instructions (no untrusted data), and a quarantined LLM that processes untrusted data without seeing user instructions. The planner emits a typed plan; the quarantined LLM only fills in data fields. Variables pass between them only with explicit permission. This structural isolation makes many prompt-injection attacks structurally impossible.
why_it_matters: CaMeL is one of the stronger structural approaches to prompt-injection defense. A reference architecture worth understanding.

title: AI Safety Institute / NIST AI RMF
short_definition: NIST's AI Risk Management Framework — a voluntary structured approach to identifying, assessing, and mitigating AI system risks used increasingly by US regulators.
full_explanation: The NIST AI RMF (Jan 2023) defines a process: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage. It is voluntary but cited in federal procurement, state laws, and EU AI Act-aligned guidance. The AI RMF Generative AI Profile (2024) specializes it for LLMs. Companies deploying AI in regulated contexts typically need to demonstrate AI RMF-consistent practices. Mirrored by the UK AI Safety Institute (AISI) and EU AI Office.
why_it_matters: For any regulated US deployment, familiarity with AI RMF is essentially required. The reference framework for responsible AI deployment.

title: EU AI Act
short_definition: The European Union's comprehensive AI regulation (2024) that classifies AI systems by risk tier and imposes obligations ranging from transparency to prohibitions.
full_explanation: The EU AI Act (force 2024, full compliance ~2026–2027) categorizes systems: prohibited (e.g., social scoring), high-risk (employment, credit, law enforcement — strict requirements), limited-risk (chatbots — transparency), and minimal. General-purpose AI models (LLMs) have their own disclosure and safety obligations, intensified for 'systemic risk' models above a compute threshold. Non-compliance penalties: up to 7% of global revenue.
why_it_matters: For any AI product with EU users, the AI Act is the primary regulatory framework. Compliance is a non-trivial engineering effort.

title: AI Governance
short_definition: The policies, procedures, and oversight mechanisms an organization uses to manage AI systems — from risk assessments to access controls to audit trails.
full_explanation: AI governance programs cover: inventory (what AI systems exist), risk classification (per AI RMF or similar), model/dataset approval workflows, ongoing monitoring, incident response, and third-party vendor assessment. Common frameworks: ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF, SOC 2 controls adapted for AI. Increasingly a formal function with dedicated AI governance officers at larger enterprises.
why_it_matters: Every enterprise AI deployment eventually needs governance structure. Understanding the function is part of shipping AI in regulated spaces.

title: Datasheet (for Datasets)
short_definition: A structured document describing a dataset's composition, collection process, intended uses, and known limitations — the dataset equivalent of a model card.
full_explanation: Datasheets (Gebru et al. 2018) prompt dataset creators to answer a standard set of questions: motivation, composition, collection methodology, preprocessing, uses, distribution, and maintenance. Increasingly required for responsible-AI disclosures and for navigating copyright/privacy concerns in ML training data. Related: Data Statements (Bender & Friedman) for NLP datasets specifically.
why_it_matters: Datasheets are becoming standard documentation for serious datasets. Worth producing for anything you share publicly.

title: Open Weights vs Open Source
short_definition: The distinction between 'open weights' (model files released, often under usage restrictions) and 'open source' (training code, data, and process also available).
full_explanation: 'Open source' in software means source code plus license freedom (OSI-approved licenses). For AI, most 'open' models are only open weights — the trained file is downloadable but training code, data, and recipes remain private. OSI's 2024 Open Source AI Definition requires releasing model parameters plus sufficient training information to recreate a similar model. True OSI-compliant open-source AI remains rare (OLMo, Pythia, some Nomic models); Llama, Qwen, Gemma, and Mistral are open-weights but not OSI open-source.
why_it_matters: 'Open source AI' marketing often overreaches. Knowing the distinction matters for reproducibility, auditability, and licensing decisions.

title: License (AI Model Licenses)
short_definition: The legal terms governing how a model's weights can be used — ranging from permissive (Apache 2.0, MIT) to restricted (Llama Community License, research-only) to custom.
full_explanation: Common AI model licenses: Apache 2.0 / MIT (fully permissive — Qwen, Mistral 7B, StarCoder), Llama Community License (permissive but some restrictions for very large deployments), Gemma Terms (permissive with Responsible AI clauses), RAIL (Responsible AI License — restricts harmful uses), research-only (CC-BY-NC, OpenRAIL), and custom 'open weights' terms with use-case prohibitions. License compatibility matters when fine-tunes inherit parent licensing.
why_it_matters: Model choice is half capability, half licensing. For commercial deployment, license diligence is mandatory.

title: Supply Chain Risk (AI)
short_definition: The risk that downloaded models, datasets, or training pipelines contain backdoors, poisoned data, or undisclosed behaviors — a growing concern as open-weights use expands.
full_explanation: AI supply chain risks: (1) poisoned training data in public corpora, (2) backdoors in community fine-tunes or LoRAs, (3) malicious code in model loading scripts (pickle vulnerabilities — one reason for safetensors), (4) compromised package registries, (5) prompt-injection payloads in third-party datasets. Mitigations: trusted model sources, safetensors format, scanning via tools like ModelScan, vendor due diligence, and preferring models with full provenance.
why_it_matters: As open-model use scales, supply-chain attacks become realistic. Governance needs to catch up.

title: AI Observability (Reprise)
short_definition: The practice of monitoring LLM and ML systems in production — traces, cost, quality, safety events, drift — to ensure reliability and continuous improvement.
full_explanation: AI observability extends traditional observability (logs, metrics, traces) with LLM-specific signals: token usage, cost per request, prompt/response content (with privacy controls), tool-use patterns, semantic drift, quality evals run over production samples, and safety-rule triggers. Platforms: Langfuse, LangSmith, Arize Phoenix, Helicone, Dynatrace/Datadog LLM observability products. OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions standardize the data model.
why_it_matters: Without observability, you cannot debug, improve, or trust LLM systems in production. Essential investment for any serious deployment.

title: LLM Router / Model Routing
short_definition: A pattern that dispatches each query to the most cost-effective model capable of handling it — cheap model for easy queries, expensive model only when needed.
full_explanation: Routers classify incoming queries (by heuristic, classifier, or LLM-as-judge) and send each to an appropriate model. Examples: simple lookup to small Mistral, complex reasoning to Claude Opus. Projects: RouteLLM, Martian, Unify, Not Diamond. Savings are often 50–90% while preserving quality on hard queries. The main challenge is building a router that does not become a worse bottleneck than the models.
why_it_matters: For high-volume LLM products, model routing is one of the biggest cost levers available.

title: Token Per Second (TPS)
short_definition: A throughput metric for LLM inference measuring generated tokens per second — the single most common latency headline number.
full_explanation: TPS comes in two flavors: TPOT (time per output token — decode speed) and TTFT (time to first token — prefill latency). Different model+hardware combos can report TPS anywhere from 20 (huge model on CPU) to 1000+ (small quantized model on Groq LPUs). Typical production ranges: 50–200 TPS for Claude / GPT-4-class on current infrastructure, 200–500 TPS for 7B-class open models on A100/H100.
why_it_matters: TPS is the headline benchmarking number for inference. Know how to read it and what bottlenecks it exposes.

title: Time to First Token (TTFT)
short_definition: The latency between sending a request and receiving the first generated token — dominated by prefill time, critical for perceived responsiveness.
full_explanation: TTFT depends on prefill cost, which scales roughly linearly with prompt length. For a 500-token prompt on H100, TTFT might be 200–500ms; for a 50k-token prompt it can be several seconds. Optimizations: prompt caching (skip the prefix), prefix caching (engine-level KV reuse), chunked prefill, and smaller models. For user-facing chat, TTFT < 500ms feels responsive, > 2s feels laggy.
why_it_matters: TTFT is what users feel as 'speed.' Optimizing it is UX-critical for interactive LLM products.

title: Cost per Query (CPQ)
short_definition: The total LLM spend for a single user interaction — including input tokens, output tokens, reasoning tokens, tool calls, and embedding lookups.
full_explanation: CPQ captures the real unit economics. Components: prompt tokens (system + history + retrieved context + user input) × input price, output tokens × output price, reasoning tokens × reasoning price, embedding calls for retrieval, and tool-call overhead. For RAG chatbots, input dominates; for reasoning agents, reasoning and output dominate. Tracking CPQ by feature is table stakes for cost management.
why_it_matters: CPQ decides whether an LLM feature can ship profitably. It is the most important product-level metric in LLM ops.

title: Chain of Density
short_definition: A summarization prompting technique that iteratively adds key entities to a summary while keeping word count constant, producing dense, information-rich summaries.
full_explanation: Chain of Density (Adams et al. 2023) asks the model to: (1) write a general summary, (2) identify missing entities, (3) rewrite at the same length with the new entities included, (4) repeat. The summary densifies over iterations without growing in length. Produces more information per word than single-pass summarization and often matches human-written summaries on entity coverage and fluency. A rare-but-powerful technique for high-stakes summarization.
why_it_matters: Chain of Density produces better summaries than single-pass prompting, especially for news and research abstracts.

title: OpenAI o4 / Frontier Reasoning
short_definition: The post-o1 reasoning-model lineage (o3, o4-mini, o4, and beyond) representing the cutting edge of LLM reasoning via RL on verifiable tasks and extended test-time compute.
full_explanation: Frontier reasoning models (2025–2026) share several architectural moves: training on RL with correctness-verifier signals (for math and code), very long internal reasoning traces, tool use integrated into the reasoning loop, and high test-time compute budgets. Performance on ARC-AGI, competition math (AIME, IMO), code contests, and scientific reasoning has climbed sharply. Open alternatives: DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-QwQ, community RL fine-tunes of Llama.
why_it_matters: Frontier reasoning is the current research frontier. Most new benchmark records come from this model class.

title: Test-Time Compute
short_definition: The paradigm of spending more compute at inference time — via longer chains of thought, best-of-N, tree search — to improve performance on hard tasks without retraining.
full_explanation: Test-time compute (TTC) is a new scaling axis alongside parameters and training data. Techniques: longer chains of thought, self-consistency over many samples, tree-of-thought, best-of-N with reward model, process-reward-model-guided search. OpenAI's o1 paper demonstrated that TTC scaling produces reliable quality gains — often matching or exceeding what more parameters would provide. This reframes inference economics: you can buy quality by spending tokens.
why_it_matters: Test-time compute is the defining concept of the reasoning-model era. Understanding it is key to planning 2026+ product features.

title: Inference Time Scaling
short_definition: The empirical observation that LLM performance on reasoning tasks continues to improve predictably as you spend more tokens or compute at inference.
full_explanation: Inference-time scaling laws (Snell et al. 2024, OpenAI o1 report 2024) show that for many reasoning tasks, accuracy improves smoothly with log of inference-time compute — mirroring the training-compute scaling laws of Kaplan and Chinchilla. Techniques: majority voting over more samples, longer CoT, PRM-guided search, and repeated refinement. Practical implication: for hard tasks, pay at inference rather than retrain from scratch.
why_it_matters: Inference-time scaling is a third axis (alongside parameters and training data) to spend on quality. A major strategic lever.

title: Reasoning Model (Category)
short_definition: An LLM specifically trained (usually via RL) to produce extended internal reasoning before answering, excelling at math, coding, and scientific problems.
full_explanation: Reasoning models diverge from standard chat models in training and behavior. They are trained with reinforcement learning on tasks with verifiable answers (math, code), learning to produce long chains of thought that significantly outperform single-shot attempts. Examples: OpenAI o1/o3/o4, Claude extended thinking mode, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-QwQ, Gemini 2 Thinking. Trade-offs: much higher cost and latency, sometimes worse general conversation quality.
why_it_matters: Reasoning models are the right tool for hard analytical tasks. Knowing when to reach for one matters for product design.

title: Open LLM Leaderboard
short_definition: Hugging Face's leaderboard that ranks open-weights LLMs on a standardized benchmark suite — the most-consulted reference for 'what open model should I try?'
full_explanation: The Open LLM Leaderboard runs submitted models through a fixed set of benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, GPQA, MATH, BBH, MuSR, IFEval in v2) and publishes results in a sortable table. Anyone can submit any model. V1 (2023) was dominated by benchmark-gamed fine-tunes; V2 (2024) uses harder, less-contaminated benchmarks. Complemented by LMSYS Chatbot Arena for preference-based ranking.
why_it_matters: The leaderboard is the default 'shopping' interface for open-weights LLMs. Read it with its known biases in mind.
